I'm in love with this game and I can't put it down on my Steam Deck OLED. I was a big fan of terraria in my young teens, and this brings me to a healthy return to that. The crafting system and combat system, as well as the armor system, are all basically the same as Terraria, but with some neat twists. It plays a lot calmer and more relaxed, however, and is great to unwind on.  A major bonus for this game for me is the NPC settlement gameplay, which has been very fun to engage with so far. I always loved minecolonies, the minecraft mod, and this feels like a healthy integration with that and the core gameplay of Terraria.  I also really dig the boss fights so far. The first boss is no "Eye of Cthulu" slow moving hitbox, but a unique boss that flies away to rain pellets from the sky at you in a bullet hell, then to return to open portals and summon enemies.    Check it out if you love modded MC, Terraria, Rimworld, or Stardew Valley!

Super solid survival crafting game, with a nice colony system to differentiate it from some of the others.  The built in Workshop support also means that basically all of my gripes with the game are easily solved with mods.  There are also plenty that expand on the base system adding things to make the game more interesting or add depth.  The world gen options and custom difficulty also mean that between the mods and built in customisation you can make your playthrough truly you own, so the only reason you wouldn't enjoy it is if you just don't enjoy this type of game.  It's also very reasonably priced and at the time of writing gets good regular updates as well as having a roadmap for the rest of this year (and stated plans for more beyond).

how did I miss this. It's legit Terraria but instead of X-Z axis it's X-Y.  It has the essential quality of life features like a button that stores everything stackable from your inventory into nearby chests that already have that item. Using a workshop draws on resources from nearby chests. Automatically pauses furnaces from burning wood if there's nothing to process. Etc.   The crafting book has a slot you can put any item into and it will show if it's part of a recipe. Exciting to realize you have the components to upgrade a trinket.  Combat is the norm for this format. Run around swinging, melee weapons have a little knockback to keep the zombies off you.   I've only killed two bosses. First mystery portal boss kicked my butt a good 3 or 4 times. Dodging multiple projectiles is tough while maintaining aim and avoiding its trash mobs.  Enjoyable! My base is poorly designed, having grown it bit by bit... time to farm and redesign. There's also settlement / recruit your own NPCs to run your settlement gameplay I've barely touched.  If you liked terraria, this game is awesome. Has references/collabs with other games in it, like a rimworld Thrumbo... a certain speechless character from Expedition 33... just so far.  /// beat the 'base' game. incursions/post-game bosses and challenges remain. I went through the game thinking the amount of trash mobs was a little much... but... I also completely ignored taking any henchmen with me... until the final 'base game' boss. Trash is instantly owned by any decently geared henchmen.. fun game.

Stardew Valley + Terraria + RimWorld  = A Marvelous Game  This game perceives like Terraria wanted to start a family with Stardew Valley and RimWorld. They had a offspring, and that newborn was handed a pickaxe, a tent, and a very big, procedurally created universe. First of all you cultivate; then, you do crafting ; then, you by accident call forth a boss who straight away reminds you that your armor plate is purely a fashion declaration, not actual protection. After that, you start strategizing, structure defenses, and positioning lanterns, acting as if you’re protecting your hometown from the tariff authorities.  This game has everything available you could possibly imagine of in this category. At first look, it might seem like just another run-of-the-mill survival game, what could totally be astonishing about that? But that’s just the first reaction. The game is beautifully improved by a finely tuned village management system. Why do we need a colony? It takes the grind off your hands (if you so opt for). You can construct a fully self-governing settlement that is absolutely self-adequate, providing its own provisions and materials, and simply drop by from time to time to sell off the excess.  As an alternative, you can generate a fully sovereign community that provides food and provides you with everything you need for your voyages (rations, healing kits), as well as ore and other riches. Or, you can construct a settlement that is totally determined by you. It all comes down to your particular liking.  The NPCs are my complete favorites. These guys work their butts off while I’m off exploring around in caverns. Occasionally i return to discover they’ve already collected the crops, smelted the ore, and are living fully comfortably unaccompanied by me. I’m beginning to have a suspicion that if I depart for too long, they’ll just vote a new chief on their own.  In Conclusion. Considering everything, Necesse is a game where you can be a man of courage, a agronomist, a town mayor, and the sufferer of a slime attack, all stuffed into one. And it’s all jolly, mellow, and full of heart. P.S. For the survival game specialists out there, let me put it plainly: this game is what would come about if Terraria, Stardew Valley, and RimWorld joined together in a passionate dance. My grading: 9/10🔚

People have gave FAR better reviews than mine/more detailed. Mine is just essentially a yap sesh LOL Got almost 66 hours into the game. If you're a fan of Terraria, Core Keeper & Stardew Valley, then you'll love Necesse. Necesse feels like a combination of those 3 games in certain ways. The end game system is.. KIND of similar to Path of Exile's mapping. You'll get tablets that'll have 1-3 mods on them. Whether it's giving the monsters more damage, tankiness or allowing you to get specific Ores/items that're only in that Tablet/map. The base building is really fun & fairly unique in the sense that enemies CAN and will destroy your base. And not repairing it will make your villagers/community feel a certain way & potentially leave. You get little quests to get specific items & even some costumes. The potion and food system is EXACTLY like Terraria's, which is really good because it'll give a sort of familiarity if you've played Terraria.   All in all, I definitely give this game a 9. I would say 10, but there's small nitpicky things that I'm not toooo fond of. But can't quite remember them due to taking a break from it once I reached essentially the end game. Got up to Tier 3 Tablets/maps & then stopped cause at that time, there wasn't much else to do. I wont go further into details about what all you can do. Mainly cause I've kind of spoiled a few things as it is. But despite it having a lottt of similarities to Terraria, Core Keeper & Stardew Valley. It does provide it's own unique touch to sandbox games. And for the price of only $15 it's totally worth it. The community on the Discord is really helpful too whenever you can reach someone. This game is absolutely something that'd be even more fun to play with like 2 more friends. So if you got any friends that enjoy sandbox games/games like this, play with em'.

If I had to pick between this and Core Keeper, it would be this.  The village management aspect makes it really fun and worthwhile to go through the effort as you end up with a small army of farmers and picker-packers that manage all your chests and crafting for you, so that you can go out adventuring while they keep you stocked up on potions, food, etc. As well as the guards who you can bring along or leave them to defend the village.  There are no skills, so you're free to swap between whatever build you like (melee, mage, range, summoner, etc.), which is great since some of the bosses are much easier depending on your build. The game also has a pretty low skill floor if you just grind for some more materials, there were only one or two bosses that I struggled with after grinding a bit.  It took me about 24hrs to reach end-game, then another 12hrs to finish it after that (kill the last boss). All-up it was a pretty fun time.  Except I never found pigs in my time playing which was strange, so I was locked out of a few of the food options.

Playing this game 2 player. 1 melee + 1 summoner. (Map difficulty Survival & Hard)  We have really enjoyed the game right through though I am finding some frustrations in the endgame due to balancing issues with classes.   Love the town building and the village creation, this is fantastic. The ability to have villagers craft and harvest and load items into chests, remove spoiled food to compost etc is amazing. Love the way the map works (though we have encountered issues getting our maps to share that we cant fix). Crafting is great, with a pretty decent variety of items and sets. I would love to have a bit more craftability in the trinkets, but it is not a problem. The useable trinket is a great touch. The overall feel of the game is great (we are playing through on a hard difficulty world from the start). I feel we got more than we payed for in this game and I would happily buy it again.  I am going to talk about the endgame balancing frustrations we have found now:  We are in the end game maps now. I am playing melee and the other player is summoner.  We are finding that summoner is greatly overpowered in the endgame and melee is very weak. All armour sets give the same base armour at the same teir at endgame, it is only the set bonus and extra stats that really vary. Melee seems geared around resilience which is very weak as it relies on hitting the enemies to build up and 1 hit from an enemy will remove max resilience and more. 100 hits with a ranged melee weapon to get max resilience (80) when enemies are doing 80-100 damage. Farming in end maps is so hard and slow as melee for the number of enemies you have to dodge and try to kill, while as a summoner you have no real care about what enemies are around, you can just run the levels with a pickaxe as enemies die off screen. Then the bosses, 90% of bosses run so far away you cant reach them with melee and by the time you get to them they have moved or are moving, so you get to tag them once or twice before resuming the chase (I am also dashing and sprinting). So there is no real way of gaining resilience or hp (Except potion), and auto resilience regen on max hp is kinda useless as each hit removes all resilience and into hp therefor no regen. Again summoner can stand on the spot dodging while everything chases the boss and crushes them down. Melee just feels redundant to be honest, but i have put so much time into upgrading the gear that it seems like such a big task to try another class. Melee seemed pretty on par with summon build through the whole game until towards the end of the deep dungeons, while it was a slower material farm it still seemed pretty strong. My issue is only at endgame. And that is my rant.  Edit: We have now finished the game (or the last boss at least), last boss was challenging but fun. I cannot imagine doing this boss as melee in single player without a summoner as well (there are many bosses i feel this way about). Items obtained after completion are pretty cool. I wish i could have obtained them before endboss. I still recommend this game, despite some balancing frustrations I enjoyed myself. Thanks!

I’m going to say this once, and only once: STOP CHASING OP/Meta BUILDS FOR COMBAT!  This game is going to burn out for you if all you care about is killing enemies fast. You will reach an end-game point where you’re so OP/Meta that there feels like nothing is left if you go this route.  With that being said….:  This game is INCREDIBLE. It gives you the fun and freedom of Stardew + Terraria, but just feels better overall. Personally I LOVE Stardew and can’t stand Terraria. Something about chasing optimal platform building to fight bosses doesn’t appeal to me; and cozy mixes of combat, settlement building/crafting, and exploration do.   Necesse is a BEAUTIFUL blend between cozy building/crafting, exploration, combat style freedom, combat in general, and overall endless experience.   Additionally we have a workshop section. Mods are convenient, compatible, detailed, and TONS of fun!   To end it off, this game is on a great path. [h1]tl;dr[/h1] [list][*]Settlement system is peak [*]Mindful of the grind [*]Highly moddable [hr][/hr] Necesse is a top-down sandbox game with an extreme amount of QoL put into it. Things that are normally tedious in other sandbox games have systems here that alleviate large chunks of what normally slows down other games. I held off on writing a review for so long simply because I didn't know how to write this when I like so many things that were done with this game.  Let's start with the NPCs. Instead of your NPCs sitting around and being generally useless, they can all perform tasks that take various amounts of tedium out of the game. You can set them to chop and replant trees for a consistent wood supply. You can have them tend your crops and you'll have a steady supply of food. Have 50 chests in your settlement? You can set item whitelists on all of them and your settlers will automatically sort all of them. This feature along sets it apart from so many of the other sandbox games, as trying to keep things organized manually often gets incredibly tedious.  NPCs can also follow you around in your party, where they can absolutely shred through enemies while you explore and gather resources. There's nothing quite like taking 5 fully-kitted NPCs into a boss fight and watching them tear it apart.  There's also the fact that the game respects your time. In something like Terraria, most bosses have that 5% drop that's important for some build. While there are still some boss items that are a random chance, any item that is equipable (weapon/trinket) is instead dropped on a rotation. That means you only have to defeat a boss a set amount of times to get all the important items, while still letting you grind kills for the rare "cosmetic" items.  There's also a quest system that orders out each boss, so you know how to progress if you don't know where to go next.  Also, the game is very open in terms of modding. If you want to change something, there's a 90% chance that you can just write something up to tweak it. I already have multiple mods published, changing and adding to multiple aspects of the game. I wrote in a durability system just for laughs. It's surprisingly easy, and I'm usually an idiot when it comes to these things.  So yeah, thumbs up from me.

I played 60+ hours after update 1.1 on Steam Deck. The game is verified on deck but I wasn’t expecting the controls to be this good on a handheld. Even without using the trackpads I was able to get everything done reasonably well and efficiently.  Many things I found annoying about Terraria have been streamlined in this game. The perspective is top-down so there is no need to deal with gravity, falling to death, or awkwardly creating single-block platforms to go back to the surface.  There are several biomes, each with a surface and cave accessible via ladders. The gameplay loop is streamlined: Go to a biome, kill its enemies, find and summon the boss, collect drops, forge stronger gear and move on to the next biome. “Hard mode” is in the form of a deeper level of caves. No annoying spreading biomes and no need to dig tunnels around them to “contain” them or anything like that.  The Elder NPC gives quests as general “kill this boss next” guidance. Just when certain things about the game start to feel tiring, a quality of life item addressing that exact thing becomes available as a quest reward. It is very well thought out and player feedback and/or gameplay data has clearly been used to make the gameplay experience smooth.  The settler and automation mechanics are really cool and well executed. Everything worked well and there was more customization than I expected. And that all of those complex mechanics could be managed efficiently without a mouse and keyboard was just incredible.  I recommend this game on Steam Deck.  Areas for improvement: (1) I killed every animal I found in a large area around my base in the early game to keep myself and settlers from starving to death. There was nothing to tell me that I would need to tame them later on to get food and resources. When it was time to domesticate them, I simply could not find any. I wish there was some small respawn rate for these animals. (2) The swamp was incredibly hard to find. All biomes including the pirate camp were reasonably close by on the map, but the closest swamp was generated about 5-6 times further than all the others. I didn’t even know that there was a swamp biome and only learned about it after reading the wiki out of frustration. It then took me 5-6 hours just to find it. World Generation 2.0 is a planned roadmap feature and I hope they would improve world gen to ensure that at least one of each biome is created reasonably nearby. (3) Crafting stations can use materials from nearby storage, but the radius defining “nearby” is not mentioned and it seems to be pretty small. Later in the game a lot of crafting and cooking resources become available and lots of chests are required to store them. I almost always ended up with chests that would be outside the nearby radius with no way to tell where that radius was, and no motivation to move them because: (4) Speaking of chests, I wish I could relocate a chest to a new place within the boundaries of my settlement together with all its contents and with settler automation rules preserved. Right now, the busywork cost of moving a chest is so high that I just ended up living with the bad chest placement choices I made early on. (5) Settlers can be really hard to find when you need them. Due to automation settlers could be anywhere doing anything at any time and since their armor sets keep changing it is impossible to remember who is who or spot them on the map. The only reasonable way to identify each settler is to give them unique cosmetics and there isn’t enough of them to identify each settler uniquely.  Constructive Advice: (1) It’s great that the Elder gives quests but I wish there was someone or something more like a guide, where I could get general gameplay tips relevant to the game stage like “Don’t kill all the cows and sheep, you may need them later” or “Legend talks about a swampy land in so-and-so direction” or “Crops benefit from fertilizer but not trees” etc. (2) I wish there was some kind of rally point where I could temporarily summon certain settlers when I needed them instead of having to find them in a sea of moving and busy NPCs doing their jobs, or waiting for them to go to sleep at night, or adding them to my party for no reason. Maybe the “guide” can be a flag or a stone or something that could give tips and also act as a rally point? (3) I wish the gameplay loop for each biome evolved or became more interesting in the deep caves and in a way unique to that biome. It was fine the first few times but became boring after a while to go through the exact same loop in each new biome so many times. Maybe add some roaming high-level champion-like enemies that could be chance encounters and drop some unique loot? It would keep things interesting in the late game.  Very excited to see where this game goes next and I wish the developers all the very best.

The town-like stuff and settlers system is a good angle, and improvements to that on roadmap look like they might be interesting later on in the year.  Aside from that, the game is okay, though feels pretty sparse currently. As many have noted, it's heavily inspired by Terraria. I think calling it a clone of Terraria is more apt, as there's similar lore/progression stuff (after beating the first boss, you start crafting with ... demon bars. Sound familiar?). A lot of the boss fights feel familiar, as well, and I'll go out on a limb here and call the early Spider Queen fight basically a reskin of Eye of Cthulhu. (I mean, that's a good fight, so if you're gonna copy one, better that than Golem I guess, right?)  Most of the journey is pretty fun, though, but this game also suffers from something that Terraria does: towards the late-game, once you've built out your town as much as possible, geared up yourself and your adventuring party to a certain point of progression, got a giant stockpile of potions and such thanks to fishing etc etc... all that's left is a giant boss-rush of whatever's left. Think post-Plantera feels.   Gear up to beat a boss, who drops gear to help you beat the next boss.  To be fair, progression through the *entire game* is based on that same loop. Terraria, too. But with both of these games, you don't really notice it while you're getting distracted by the fun side-quests that are abundant in the early game.  I found myself getting burned out by the giant worm boss (not that one, the other one... if you know, you know...). I have not checked out the post-Wizard 'incursions', because it sounds like more boss rush content to me.  (Also, while we're on the topic, but hot take: can we stop doing Worm Bosses in games like this? I'm getting Calamity flashbacks. Or maybe just limit yourself to one worm boss per game. That would be nice. This game's got 4, if you count the way the Dragon bosses function as 'worm-like')  Modding right now is pretty sparse, with maybe 4 or so worth investing in. Modding *framework* is very rough, and on my modded playthrough a single mod updating was enough to actually break my character savefile and I had to start a new character. I was working on getting him up to speed and ... burnout, again.

There is a lot to like about this game.  The base building and settler automation is like a light version of Rim World or Dwarf Fortress.  I wish more exploring, crafting, base building games had a village management system similar to Necesse.  The game boasts an infinite procedural world but the exploration is very superficial.  You can add settlers from your village to your party but they are more of a liability as they increase the difficulty disproportionally to the firepower they bring.  You can allow them to use potions and arm them with high end weapons and gear but that is tedious and it doesn't really balance out.  The game gets pretty grindy at times.  The boss fights add some variety but I found the fights infuriating in most cases.  Often times I struggled to beat a boss until I obtained a new piece of gear that allowed me to win.  Of course people will say get gooder or "I beat every boss on the highest level with no challenge so it's not the game's fault."  However, the achievement for beating the final boss shows only 1.7% of players completed this.  I only did so because I dropped the difficulty to casual.  It's a plus that the game has difficulty settings.  I had to use it on 2 bosses because their difficulty was so out of step with every other boss.  In the end I wasn't able to play the game the way I wanted to.  I wanted to play a summoner build but that build was very underpowered.  So I tried a ranged build but that was frustrating to have to manage ammo and I never got that build to work either.  I tried melee and found that the best melee weapons act like ranged weapons.  The game knows that you need to play a ranged build to advance so it gave melee a ranged option.  That speaks to the overall balance of the game which I found all over the place.  It was a bit jarring.  Overall I played the game for quite a while, I had fun, and the game showcased some innovative design with some poor design and pacing at times.  All in all a good game and I got more than my money's worth out of the game.  Cheers.

Necesse is a strong sandbox survival game that earns a solid 8/10, especially for players who enjoy base-building and exploration.  The game combines elements of survival, crafting, and colony management, which makes it stand out. You’re not just surviving on your own—you can build a settlement, assign tasks to villagers, and grow your base over time. This adds a lot of depth and keeps gameplay interesting.  Exploration is another highlight. The world is large and randomly generated, with different biomes, enemies, and bosses to discover. There’s always something new to find, which gives the game good replay value.  Combat is simple but works well enough, though it’s not the main focus. I started playing this game in early access (Before the plains biome got added) It was already a good game then.  So I think I played this game enough to leave review as a regular Rimworld, Starsector Battle Brothers, and Stardew Valley player.    Positives:  -Prices to value ratio is pretty good. I am cheap so this is a big plus for me.  -Fun atmosphere  -The beginning is extra fun and relaxing. Also verry exciting.  -Music  -Automate everything. Except some end game stuff.  -Crafting, gears, building and food varieties. Many options to choose from.  -Combat is very flexible. Many options to choose from as well and you can even build your own Death Squads. Add as many NPC as you want.  -Other cool things like mounts and funny things you find in the world  -The boss fights and unique biomes are fun. And the different layers to the biomes is a refreshing experience.    Negative  -Boss fights are very annoying at times. Some bosses keep shifting and moving around. And in the end game they hit way too hard. Way harder than in early access.   Some bosses are damn near impossible to hit consistently if you have a melee build that doesn't use melee weapons with long range even then it's still annoying.  Combined that with the speed increase they get when near death and you have recipe for a bad day.  -It gets way too grindy in the end game. I got bored at times.  -The boss fights get repetitive in the end game.   -If you don't follow a certain path to upgrade your gears you will reach a bottle neck.  -Raiders can be insanely overpowered in the end game.    Necesse is very fun game. I always come back to it every year or so just for the nostalgia. It offer something close to terraria but not Rimworld or Stardew valley.  Despite all the negatives I mentioned, you can tweak the difficulty to your liking.    If you like terraria, Rimworld or even Stardew valley you will have some fun with this game.   The other have more depth but Necesse offer something unique.   I would buy this game on sale or full price. It's worth it 100%.

Necesse is a masterpiece that will only get better with time. With so many different ways to play, you're bound to find something that's fun for you; you like killing bosses? Great, there are 20 different ones to find and fight. You prefer a more relaxed building game? There are a ton of building materials that mesh really well together, allowing you to make whatever comes to mind. You want to turn it into a farming game? Why not? The settlers will even harvest and replant for you.    You may have heard that this game is exactly like Terraria, and while they do have a lot of similarities, they also differ in many ways. Don't come into Necesse expecting it to be Terraria; rather, come expecting another take on the same genre.    Necesse also has an awesome team that actually listens to the community, a rare thing nowadays. I've played through the game in so many different ways, and every time it feels new and exciting. Necesse is my personal favorite game, and I would suggest it to anyone who likes this style of game.

What can I say? Necesse is a perfect blend of cozy sandbox resource gathering and crafting, mixed with hardcore combat elements such as area specific bosses. It is one of those rare games that somehow manages to feel peaceful and intense at the same time, and that balance is what kept pulling me back into it night after night.  There is something incredibly satisfying about the simple rhythm of the game. You start off cutting down trees, mining stone, and gathering whatever resources you can find. Before long you are crafting better tools, organizing your storage, and slowly shaping a settlement that begins to feel like home. Watching your little village grow and seeing settlers move in gave me a genuine sense of pride. It feels like the world is growing with you.  What really hooked me though was how the calm moments slowly give way to real danger. The game does not just let you stay comfortable forever. Each new biome and dungeon pushes you to prepare better gear, rethink your strategy, and face bosses that can absolutely wreck you if you walk in unprepared. Those fights feel meaningful because they are the result of everything you have been building up to. When you finally beat one after struggling with it, the feeling is incredibly rewarding.  Exploration in Necesse also has a special charm to it. Sailing to new islands and discovering different environments always gave me that sense of curiosity that great sandbox games capture so well. Every trip out into the unknown felt like it mattered because there was always something new to find or something dangerous waiting for me.  The pixel art is simple but full of personality, and it fits the atmosphere of the game perfectly. It makes the world feel warm and inviting while still leaving room for moments of chaos during combat. The more time I spent in Necesse, the more attached I became to the little systems that keep everything running.  For me, Necesse is one of those games that quietly sneaks into your routine. You tell yourself you will just play for a little while to gather some resources or finish building something, and suddenly hours have passed. It has that rare quality where the world starts to feel like your world. That is something special, and it is why this game has stuck with me the way it has.

While I think Core Keeper has a much stronger visual style, Necesse provides the same gameplay loop significantly expanded. The world is separated into 3 levels stacked on top of each other. The Overworld is generally the safest and easiest to navigate, then the caves are where the majority of the early game boss fights and crafting materials are found. Lastly, you have the depths underneath the caves for your late game materials and bosses. Layering the world like that gives it more life and logic over Core Keeper's completely subterranean world. The boss fight progression feels very much like Terraria and in a similar vein has a very challenging end game path to keep things going.     Settlement building is relatively simple compared to other games of the type. It's very easy to build up a large community, and only requires a little planning and effort to really maximize the rewards. Bigger houses with more furniture, and automating gourmet food items through farming are all it takes to make things optimal.     Some things about the UI are a little clunky. As you progress, you'll unlock pouch items that serve as additional inventory for specific item types. When you pick these items up from the ground, they automatically go into these pouches if there is room. When you open a chest though, shift clicking them does NOT. Similarly, void pouches can hold any item, but don't seem to automatically stack items you pick up from the ground or chests if there's already one in the void pouch. Lastly, it's a little cumbersome placing objects facing the correct way without a rotate button. You have to face the direction you want the object to face. For example, if you want a bookshelf against the northern wall of your house to face south, you must turn south yourself before placing it, otherwise it will be facing north. That's not a huge issue, but it's way more annoying with making minecart tracks that intersect. As far as I can tell, you can only have the rail turn to the intersection from one direction, meaning you have to pass the turn an turn around if coming from the other direction. Not a huge deal, just annoying.     I've enjoyed Necesse a lot overall. It's as challenging as it needs to be without being too much of a grind early on. Settlement building is a great optional mechanic. World travelling is pretty quick with minecarts, teleports and ladders to the mines and depths matching the coordinates of the overworld. A lot fo the burdens of survival crafting games have been simplified to keep you in the parts that are fun.

I'm into the survival genre, and Necesse doesnt have the 3D graphics, but the rest is there... mix it in with some minecraft.. definitely had a great 70hours, I did most as co-op (you can kind play solo then go back and redo quests as coop too, it all works), every QoL is in this game also.. a range of fast travel, automated chests etc.  Lots of builds/weapons/armour-sets to collect.. there is a lot of diversity in how you take on bosses.. though not all as effective, but many of them will work.. there is quite a lot of depth in there with buffs and food, enchants, you get quite a lot of trinkets to mix and match to make your build work for the given encounter (and the boss encounters do encourage different gearsets).. its good stuff.  On top of all that its an automation sim of sorts.. everything can be done automatically by your villagers.. you can even grab a bunch of them, put a healing staff on them and use them to make fights a lot more in your favour.. its a bit of a pain but it really helps.. the game is tuned on the tougher side. There is an endgame system also which is a nice touch, its quite a clever system but not perfect but it will give you something to work towards if you dont want to put it down.  If devs do read these, my main suggestions are that the order of the biomes early on doesnt feel great as there is no obvious order to them but you need the exact pickaxe in order to proceed, maybe make the next biome in sequence part of the questline.  And endgame, I can see the vision.. it goes so close.. the incursions end up too long though.. doing laps.. scouring for the mobs or ore, compounded by rly difficult bosses (the snake HP in particular is so out of whack), it sucks the fun out of what would otherwise be a really cool endgame sequence. It felt like I was grinding my bu*t off for every scrap and material and upgrade.. but then at some point you finally get 1 set sort of working.. then suddenly you have everything you could ever need but no real need for the materials, i.e. its not a smooth ride. I realised late in end game that I could just turn the difficulty down to make the bosses less painful, wish Id realised that earlier.. but anyway it was still a lot of fun! Oh.. and with incursions, the cavelings should have like 1% of the HP they do.. couple of shots should kill them, I stopped bothering when it was taking 20 or 30 chases per caveling.

[h1]The best in its genre for top-down view style right now![/h1]    Any game that combines "base-building survival" with "colony management" will always have a special place in my heart. I bought Necesse a long time ago because it matched my preferences, but the game looked clunky at that time, so I never actually tried playing it.    When they announced the 1.0 release, I got curious and decided to give it a try. To be honest, Necesse doesn’t really do anything completely new. We’ve seen 2D art styles like this before, and most of its gameplay mechanics can already be found in many games on the market nowadays.    But what makes Necesse great is how well it combines all of those mechanics into one game. It has survival elements like hunger and climate, base building, colony management, villager needs (beds for sleeping, food for hunger), crafting, farming, resource gathering, and villagers can even help with your work. There’s zoning, trading, animal husbandry, fishing, mining, raiding, dungeons, and on top of all that, online co-op.    There’s just so much going on in this game. Right now, it’s the best in its genre when it comes to a top-down horizontal view style. If you’re looking for something similar but in a side-view vertical style, try "Ratopia". And if you prefer a 3D perspective, I can easily recommend the "Dragon Quest Builders 1 and 2" series.    For me, this game can easily replace "First Feudal". I’m not going to compare it with "Core Keeper", since that game doesn’t really focus on colony management.    It took me 200 hours to finish the game with 100% achievements. There were moments when I felt bored because of the grind for achievements, but other than that, there was never a dull moment.

As someone who really likes Terraria and RimWorld, I really enjoyed playing this game.    Yes, it takes elements from both these games and combines them. Is it better than either of them? No, but it's similar enough to Terraria, and enough of an interesting spin on RimWorld that if you like either of those games, I recommend you at least give Necesse a try, you might end up liking it.

Went into this game not expecting much except for it being liek Rimworld. Got real surprised at what this game is about and I gotta say, I'm officially addicted to this game for the duration of the content available.  Necesse is a game with Terraria gameplay + Corekeeper Exploration + Rimworld settlement management. It does it in a way that's engaging compared to the first 2 which I have played.  Pro: - Gameplay: The game plays like terraria with the combat and loot. It's fun with the tools you fight with, the loot is exciting to find with some having prefix modifers to make it better or worst. But why not compare it to corekeeper, corekeeper combat gameplay is more casual and not as engaging as terraria. People even call Necesse the sequel to Terraria. - Exploration: The world is huge "SHUT ♥♥♥♥♥♥", there's lots to explore, plenty og biomes, and the way the game does exploration is clever with ladders going into underground areas that mirror the overworld, and alternate ways to go other areas of interest. There's villages and points of interest that you can find to find cool loot. - Settlements: This aspects really separates the game from terraria and Corekeeper. In all 3 games you can recruit settlers to give you benefits, but what Necesse does that's better is you can have your settlers work for you to gather resources like fishing, farming, mining, getting rare items, and even joining you in battle to fight. I absolutely love this design, hope other games follow it to make settlers useful and make all hte tedious task automated.  - Coop: You can play it with a lot of players and can even rent a cheap server from them for $9.99 to have  a persistent worlding going. I am definitely considering purchasing a world. - Content: At the time of being introduced to the V1.0, there is a lot of content to find and do. There's even quests you can do to work and build towards. Lots fo different types of bosses to find all of which are generally unique to each other. - Progression: I like that as you progress through the game, the game actually gives you good rewards that further your progression in the game and improve your quality of life, such as getting teleporter items to return home easier, container items to help you hold more items, and just overall useful items to help you. - Music: Most of the music is great, I enjoy a lot of them and you can even get a record player to put in your base, and an mp3 player to play the music you enjoy listening to.  Meh: - Hitbox: The hitbox is wonky, but I'm not sure if it's because you connect to a server to play so you don't have perfectly accurate hitboxes. Because a good consistent couple of times. what looks like I should have avoided the attack, I get hit. - Item collecting: I would have really liked if when you find an item you don't own in your collection it would give an icon to tell you "You never picked up this item before" so I don't have to constantly keep opening my item list to see what I'm missing. it's very tedious. - Customization: Could have more customization options, and the ability to recolor the armour and clothes in the game.  CON: - The difficulty design: Oh "let's just make the enemies does more damage and have more health." That is prime lazy design. What high difficulty should have done is introduce hidden moves that don't show up in lower difficulties. So instead of challenging the players on higher difficulties. It's just who can tank longer and kill the other. My one big complaint in this game.   Overall, I really enjoyed this game and played long sessions. That shows how much I enjoy the game. I hope more content gets added, but maybe not before I finish collecting everything. I highly recommend Necesse. it's a Necesse-city to play it.

Necesse is a top-down build/craft/fight game in the vein of Terraria or Core Keeper. Along with the traditional aspects present in these games, it adds a "colony management" mechanic in which NPCs can be recruited to your base and assigned tasks to perform.    In terms of UI, Necesse is quite good. Sound and music are acceptable and well-varied, though you likely won't have the soundtrack stuck in your head afterward. Graphically the game is a 16-bit style 2D cartoon. More importantly, it's mostly clear, though some areas are overly dark and things can get rather hectic. Controls are tight and customizable; they quickly become second nature and get out of your way.    Gameplay is divided into roughly four parts: exploration/gathering, base-building, NPC automation, and combat. The exploration/gathering is very color-by-numbers, featuring the same pickup and mining mechanics present in all the games Necesse was so clearly inspired by, but it worked in those games and it works here, too. Base-building is also largely familiar, though enemy invasions are finally interesting (unlike, e.g. Terraria or Grounded), which is a real plus. Base efficiency (navigability, defendability, etc.) is also much more important than other games, because of the NPC automation aspect. This is the most novel thing Necesse offers, and it's hit-or-miss. In the end, we found it lacked real challenge/tradeoffs and was mostly a distraction, but the foundation is there for it to be much more, and there certainly are opportunities to make your life easier via NPCs. If our group had engaged with these systems earlier we might have progressed more quickly and enjoyed them more, but progress was so fast early on we never got around to it. Finally, there's the combat. This is exactly what you'd expect, though fairly difficult solo where a single death resets boss fights (multiplayer is more forgiving as slain players can rejoin after 30s if the party isn't fully wiped). Weapon/armor/build variety is decent, though certainly not Path of Exile, and enemies and bosses are varied and challenging.    All told, then, Necesse is a varied, well-crafted, and well-paced game with solid presentation that doesn't overstay its welcome. Its most novel feature is somewhat rough, but if you enjoyed the games that inspired it, Necesse won't disappoint.

[h1]Terraria meets RimWorld – A Total Time-Sink![/h1] [h1]Played: 53+ hours (Version 1.1)[/h1]  I bought [i]Necesse[/i] while I was stuck at home sick, and honestly, it was love at first sight. I’ve already poured over 53 hours into it within a single week. If you’re looking for a game that perfectly blends the exploration and progression of [i]Terraria[/i] with the colony management of [i]RimWorld[/i], this is it.  [h2]The Gameplay Loop[/h2] I play exclusively in single-player, and the sense of progression is spot on. You start with nothing but basic gear, head into your first cave, and slowly gather materials to tackle the next biome. On the surface, what starts as a simple house quickly evolves into a bustling village.  [h2]Management & Efficiency[/h2] What hooked me was the settler system. Assigning tasks and watching your village grow is incredibly satisfying. In fact, I loved it so much that after getting pretty far in my first world, I started a second one just to apply everything I’d learned. My goal now? Building the most efficient settlement possible from the ground up.  [h2]Depth & Longevity[/h2] Even after 53 hours, I feel like I’ve barely scratched the surface. There’s still so much to discover and so many bosses to conquer. The game feels surprisingly polished for being "fresh" on the market in its 1.1 version.  [h2]Pro Tip for New Players[/h2] Don't make my mistake—don't try to do everything alone! Equip your settlers with decent gear and bring them along to boss fights. It makes a world of difference and turns a difficult struggle into a manageable (and fun) battle.  [h2]Verdict[/h2] Highly addictive and atmospheric. Whether you’re into dungeon crawling or base building, [i]Necesse[/i] delivers on both fronts. I can’t wait to see what else I’ll find in this world!

This game by itself is like a 8/10. Compare it to Terraria, which this game seems to draw a lot of inspiration from, it's like a 6/10. The game does a lot of things right but fails in some  areas where Terraria just made it better.   I played this game with two other friends and man we had a blast. I mainly played as a ranger and my friends played mage and melee/summoner respectively. There are four classes in the game with different weapon and armor types for each class. There are also trinkets that you can equip that buffs you in different ways, like increasing your fire rate by X% for an example. To me this gets quite stale as I don't find these sort of buffs interesting since they don't change the way you play the game.  Weapon progression is also boring and unsatisfying since every new weapon you get just feels like your previous one. I thought I was the only one with this issue but my friends expressed a similar dissatisfaction with their weapon progressions as well.   Exploring the world is... alright. There are like 4 different biomes with it's own enemies and theme. Each biome also has it's own underground where you can fight more enemies, sub-biomes, mine ores and materials, among other things.  The pacing of gathering materials and getting better gear is terrible and easily my least favorite thing about the game. When unlocking a "next phase" of the game world you can immediately start collecting materials to get the best armor and gear available. So there's little reason to get the next tier armor when you instead could get another armor that's maybe 2 times better than that with little to no more work.  Bosses seems to also suffer from this since you're meant to kill around 3 bosses to unlock the next "world phase" but since you've probably gotten the best armor and gear possible at that point of the game the first 2 bosses are just cakewalks. The game would've benefited if it went to a similar approach to Terraria or Valheim where new materials used to upgrades are locked behind the boss battles themselves.   The bosses themselves are fun however! Every boss is unique to each other with different gimmicks, aesthetics and music. Bosses have their own drop pools with different items, usually weapons which are for the most part worth grinding for.  I won't get more into the bosses for any new players that might be reading this.   The thing that makes this game stand out from others within the same genre is the settlement system, which is very fun, almost addicting. I loved building homes for each settler, micromanaging their work and optimising my settlement. The point of the settlement system is basically to do labor for you, like ggathering wood and sorting chests, so you can focus on other stuff. Also just watching and seeing how your settlement has grown and watching your settlers running around doing their tasks is very satisfying.

If you like Rimworld and Terraria, then you’ll love this game. Settlement building is addictive, exploring and dungeon crawling is thrilling. I can’t wait to see where future updates take this game.   Getting in now feels like discovering Terraria in 2011, the foundations for a generational game are here.   Things I’d like to see added: - Even more in depth settlements. Right now villagers just feel like workers, and maintaining a village is very easy. I’d love to get to a point where you need to properly manage them: defining schedules, ensuring they have enough free time, creating designated dining rooms and games areas, more settler roles, more punishing food requirements, etc.   - Further expansions on the wiring system. Wiring is quite bare bones currently, we have all these logic gates and cool features to basically just open doors and shoot arrows. The possibilities are endless here: pistons for complex doors and circuits, conveyer belts to move items, fans to push monsters, hoppers/chutes to pick up items, integration with mine carts, and so much more.  A further point here is that “wires” feel a bit out of place to me. This game is much more medieval in nature, and having electric wires feels incongruent with the exiting world building. It would be cool if they reworked the wiring system to be more mechanical/magic inspired, rather than electricity inspired  Now that being said, I still highly recommend this game. The fundamental foundations and identity of Necesse are impeccable. This game has nowhere to go but up, buy it now.

Hours played: “Enough to question my life choices (in a good way)”  Necesse is what happens when a survival game, a colony sim, and an action RPG lock themselves in a room and come out as something dangerously addictive.  You start with a stick and dreams. You end up running a massive settlement with dozens of settlers, elite guards in endgame armor, automated farms, kitchens that still can’t cook fast enough, and Incursion bosses that actively try to turn you into paste.  And the best part? Every time you think you’ve figured Necesse out, it politely waits until nightfall… and then sends raiders in better gear than you.  What Necesse Does Brilliantly  ✔️ Incursions: Some of the best endgame content I’ve played. Each boss feels like a puzzle, not a DPS check. ✔️ Settlement management: Your settlers are helpful, needy, and occasionally die horribly. You will care. ✔️ Progression: Constant, meaningful upgrades. Tier climbing feels earned, not handed out. ✔️ Boss fights: From the Night Swarm to the Sunlight Champion, these fights demand learning, not luck. ✔️ Updates: Version 1.0 was huge. 1.1 said “hold my drink” and added more chaos.  Things That Will Happen to You  • You will say “one more Incursion” and lose three hours • You will underestimate a boss and pay for it emotionally • You will lose settlers and feel genuine guilt • You will finally win a fight with two hearts left and feel invincible • You will immediately die to the next boss  Final Verdict  Necesse doesn’t just reward curiosity — it dares you to survive your own ambition.  If you enjoy:  Terraria-style progression  RimWorld-lite settlement management  Boss fights that punish panic  Games that respect your time while absolutely consuming it  Then Necesse is not optional. It’s mandatory.  Buy it. Build a settlement. Lose it. Rebuild it better.  👍 Yes, I recommend this game.

TLDR: Play it if you don't mind weird balancing choices, lack of real class freedom and you are seeking a new terraria-like.  Let me start by saying the game is decent in some ways great even but in some others it falls terribly flat.  This game tries to play with the terraria formula and to its credit it manages to improve on it on some ways but gets the basics wrong in many ways in my opinion (more on that later).  So at its core it is a game with an open world and a cave system which is a separate but also open world. For the most part the overworld being easier much like in terraria. While spelunking you can find different biomes, minibiomes and chests with treasure and trinkets, so far all very familiar. Some of those trinkets can also be combined. All of this is great in my opinion. After a few bosses you get access to a second cave world which acts as this game's version of the terraria hardmode, with harder bosses on variations of the same previous biomes and some more trinkets.   There are 2 things that I would say necesse does in all of that, that improve massively on the terraria formula (well, one of them does). The first one is settler work and automation. Being able to set up work orders and farms for them to run and even trade makes all the grinding for materials and potions tedium free. You set it up and whenever you need them, they will be ready, waiting for you in the assigned chest. The second... does not work so well. You can make the settlers explore with you. Their AI is pretty bad. If you equip them with the highest gear you have access to and potions, they will do ok but will be a resource sink that is simply not worth it. Each settler going with you on expeditions increases enemy hp which is often higher than their dps output without those potions to let them survive some hits. The later in the game you are the worse this will be since many bosses have very punishing mechanics that the npcs straight up ignore and will get nuked.  All in all the settler companions are interesting but are not executed properly.  Now to my issues with the game. Some of them have seriously made me consider putting the game down and playing something else entirely.  The boss balancing is absolutely atrocious. Many bosses are much harder than the next couple bosses by a mile. To make it even worse, many of them require particular builds or weapon types. (Again, more on that later). This makes the progression feel incredibly whacky.  Class balancing. The weapons in this game are for the most part serviceable, not incredibly unique (with some exceptions) but good. That being said... not all classes are created equal. I think in a game like this, freedom to run whatever build type you want is key. Note that I did not say any armor or any trinket combination but build type aka magic, range, melee or summoning. That won't work in most difficulty levels.   There are several bosses that require you to either perform perfectly for a very very long time or use piercing damage which is the intended route. That is perfectly fine except... oops you cant do that with some damage types.   This lack of balancing between classes forces you to either struggle much more than you should in some key parts of the game or swap playstyles completely which feels wrong.  The end game mechanic (incursions) is fun. I like the modifiers, multi boss fights, the rewards, scaling difficulties and more than anything, the ability to choose any armor and weapon in the game and upgrade them to full end game gear.  Some of the incursion bosses are the same "hardmode" bosses (called deep caves in necesse) and some are entirely new. Cool but as you can imagine, these too are not properly balanced. You will stomp the slime boss without looking but sunlight champion will nuke you. Then the moon boss will die if you breathe and so on. All being the same difficulty tier.   This type of game is about making your character, finding your playstyle and progressing consistently after struggling not having extreme peaks and valleys in difficulty.   Overall, the game is ok is not the best terraria-like out there by a long shot but will do if you want something new. That being said, be prepared to deal with very strange difficulty curves.

(SOME MINOR SPOILERS AROUND LATER GAME MECHANICS) First things first, in my opinion, the trailers don't do this game justice, there is so much to it! I honestly didn't think I'd enjoy it because of the art style and a number of other things but wow am I glad I got it on sale!  Necesse scratches so many gamer itches I have. I grew up playing bullet hell games (like RotMG) and the combat, especially boss fights, scratch that itch. I enjoy Rim World management and the settlement/villager system really scratches that itch while not only optimizing item management/acquisition, but also combat if you equip them appropriately. Also, I love! decorating and there is so many options and opportunities to really decorate your settlement and make it your own.   I didn't like Terarria or Minecraft but I loved this game. Pros: - Great combat, almost any play style is viable though building resilience really is recommended imo. - So many "wow" moments when using new mechanics and fighting new bosses. - Tons of optimization mechanics in item management as well as combat/farming, - So many tools to make monotonous tasks easier if not automated.   Cons: - The importance of building a squad of settlers to follow you into combat is not really... impressed upon the player. It make so many fights/exploring new areas easier. I didn't realize this until LATE game. This needs to be more directly presented to players sooner - The mechanics around dying with a squad are annoying, I wish I could build a squadron and press a single button to have them follow me. - The game doesn't make it clear which buffs/item or armor special abilities are usable by settlers (seems like most all are). But, it's not obvious and contributed to me not using them for awhile as setting up settlers with gear, potions and food is kind of a pain innitially.  - Easier ways to set up mob farms would be nice.... - More healing options would also be nice... I've put extensive time into the game and have only found 2 viable methods. Though this would effect balancing I suppose... - While I appreciate the "wow" moments of finding a new item that makes exploration/gameplay easier. I think the early game can feel VERY slow and that needs to be addressed. It's not obvious all the tools that will eventually become available to players to automate/speed things up. This could be resolved by a Item menu showing progress to these items and description of their benefits (IE: Bulletin Board w/ special settlers, Portals, the fact that items can be tiered up).   Great game, buy it, spend hours lost and having fun.

Fun and automation-focused open-world survival crafting game that deeply respects the players time and doesn't ask for hours of grinding.    With a constant sense of progression lined with world exploration, automation, and discovery and upgrade tasks along the way, Necesse outshines its contemporaries by focusing on a fun experience that doesn't get stale during its runtime.    If games like Valheim and Enshrouded feel boring, hollow, and just too much of a slog to you, give this one a go. It feels like at every step the developers asked themselves: "Is this fun, or is this just padding the runtime without further purpose." and always chose to give you automation options to skip the grind. All the usual time wasters of cooking food, crafting potions, and tending to crops are automated with a delightful UI that doesn't get in the way of building a nice home base. In the same vein, dungeons drop ample amounts of buff and health potions and don't require tedious preparation.    Multiplayer is seamless, and it's fun to both complete tasks together and do separate things.    Combat is, for the most part, fair and predictable, with bosses being on the easier side of things while getting challenging quickly when gear progression is skipped.     This is a gem of a game and a great example of pacing done well.

Recommended for the base building, and the initial exploration of the systems (crafting, profiteering, settlement management).    Not recommended for the achievements, boss restricted loot, and boss design.  The Complete Collector Achievement (see below) adds 40-80 hours on a dedicated character.  The Bosses attack patterns will limit build diversity (often favoring ranged/mobile builds) and the default arenas work against you (e.g. Queen Spider).  [h2]The game[/h2]  You mine/lumberjack to craft, to base build, to refine, to equip, to kill bosses to unlock the next tier.   This flow is repeated 7 times (bosses), 6 more times (deep bosses) and then shrunk down to a minified version in incursions (with 7 more bosses and some additional repeats).  There are diversions and you can skip tiers at various point for equipment (e.g. glaive or runebound).  There are Trade (Pawn Brokers), base management, wiring/trap system (so close to a tower defense game), party management (take settlers with you, note it will scale a bosses hp), raids against you, farming, cooking and animal husbandry. None of these feel incomplete or bad but there are no shortages of improvements players could want.  [h2]Complete Collector" Achievement[/h2]  If there is an achievement which will break the will of a player and flip their recommend to not-recommend it is this achievement.  You will wiki everything.  You town will turn into pig meat processing factory.  You will hate tic-tac-toe. You will wonder why traps require drops vs crafting (or may even have to restart since raids outscale them).  Your final thoughts may drift to: why is so much of this content not available earlier in the game? hundreds of items/trinkets/armors/decorations.  The character that did this was: 2d23h old.  [h2]Tips[/h2]  * Majestic Pig Statue (100% achievement) requires lots of Pigs. * Visit each overland biome early. * Swamp Adventure Quest for a helpful early trinket. * Dungeons for trap farming. * Trap Cavelings with walls you bring. * All (or nearly) weapons and armor can be upgraded once you reach Incursions. * Design a Stockpile Room. So Settlers are there, not around you when sorting. * Create a "Not my problem box".  Disallow everything. Let Settlers sort it.  [h2]"Extreme" End Game and its Flaws and Suggestions[/h2]  These only show up once you break a system. Examples are "constant raids" (with 50+ Challenger Flags while at Incursion 10), or "farm produces more then you use", or have ton of settlers.  You will hit many of these attempting to 100% the achievements.  Individual tolerances will vary.  * Inventory management and the Void Bag (pocket).   Revival Potions and Ascended Shards (among others) are invisible to crafting/dialogs but yet drop on death (default option).  So you either have to manage them, or lose inventory space.  Another example are Coins won't go into a Coin Purse in the Void Bag.  Ultimately this means that your standard 40 slot inventory becomes "smaller" over time leading to more click/destroy. * Void Shards as Ammo (e.g. Void Bullets). You will have explorers 100% of the time on Dungeon Explorations just to keep up with Ammo needs, and you will likely fall behind. * Where the F is item X. You will likely create a highest priority chest for your Settlers to sort/find an item.  While the chest filtering system exists if you are sorting sugar beets which settlers spread across 5 fridges somewhere the design failed. * Forbid Zones/Items. It is easier to simply wall off a section of your base (in my case pork fridges and weapons) vs use the tools.  Settlers can ignore forbidden areas. * Diet/Equipment Management. Unlike Chests, searching (e.g. Pork) in the Diet system doesn't expand tabs. * Party Inventory management without a Party (remove only).  Food expires so you constantly have to refill it and it shouldn't require a Party.  * It is very easy to accidentally undo a Settler Party preset (groups 1-6). * Traps don't scale. I suppose if I farmed for dozens of hours I could build a maze that could deal with +10 level incursion raiders, but I am unwilling to try. * Banners.  It is unclear while Challenger Banners are placed, and a Speed Banner is placed in a Banner Stand.  It is more unclear why they can be destroyed by the Final Boss (the likely only reason players would use them). * Create a 50x Challengers Banner recipe.  Removing/replacing this level of raiding should be easier (at this point players have everything). Have a start condition where your only settlement flag is a 50x Challenger Banner. * A "collected" or "not collected" on the top tip (mostly for the 100% collection achievement but is equally valuable to those who buy costumes from Settlers/Exotic visitors. * Settlers Popup-menu. Due to crafting resource stockpiles and crafting station distance restrictions settlers will likely congregate to a small area of your base, and you will misclick, perhaps frequently until you redesign a hub (stockpiles) and spokes (crafting stations).  I definitely enjoyed my time and will see what changes and/or mods exist in the future.

This game is absolutely fantastic – and it’s a perfect match for the Steam Deck! The charming pixel art style looks great on the handheld’s screen, and the game runs smoothly without any issues.   The village system is one of the highlights. Farming, animal care, and survival mechanics are refreshingly straightforward, so you don’t get bogged down in micromanagement. This frees you up to focus on exploring dangerous caves and mining valuable resources for crafting gear and upgrades. As your village expands, villagers take on dedicated roles like farming, forging equipment, patrolling for safety, or even organizing your inventory, and when you head out to explore, you can take them with you to fight enemies by your side. Plus, random raids will occasionally target your village, so you’ll need to defend your home and your colony, adding an extra layer of excitement and purpose to keeping things strong. Watching everyone work together makes the whole place feel like a living, thriving community.   Combat is fast-paced and satisfying against regular enemies, but boss fights are where the action really shines. Each boss features unique designs and challenging mechanics, plus they drop powerful special items and weapons. Progression feels rewarding as you take down bosses to unlock new areas – these locations offer new materials that let you craft better gear, which in turn lets you tackle even tougher foes and challenges.   For endgame content, incursions are outstanding – they’re intense, well-designed challenges that put all your village preparations and hard-earned equipment to the test. There’s also plenty of item variety to support different playstyles, and a robust modding scene to customize and enhance your experience further.   My only minor gripe is that the sound can be inconsistent at times – it doesn’t always function as intended. But for its price point, the game delivers incredible value, and exciting future updates (including a planned marriage system) are already in the works. It would be a great addition if villagers eventually had distinct personalities with unique likes, dislikes, or traits to help players form deeper connections with them.   Rating: 8/10 – Highly recommended for Steam Deck owners and survival-craft fans!

Played this as a group with 3 other friends that I regularly play survival games with.    Really like this game - artisticly and progression feels like Terraria but top down instead of side scroller. Mining progression feels similar, itemization feels similar and bosses some what feel similar. I like how big the world is and how you can find bosses in different biomes by either summoning them or doing something else specific to the biome.     In terraria the world progresses, however in this the world itself doesn't progress much, you can keep exploring farther out, I'm not sure if there is a limit, but I do think the biomes would keep repeating themselves (new biomes come with the new content). While the world doesn't progress too much, finishing bosses does unlock new items and colonists that can change how you play the game.    Colonists is where there is a lot more depth here over Terraria, lit takes reference from Rimworld and Dwarf fortress - if you have played either of those games you will feel very familiar in how you command them here. It allows you to automated a lot of things like farming, hunting, etc. You can even send traders to sell items for you or send people out on adventuring parties. Another cool thing is you can take any colonist in to your party to help you while adventuring or in boss fights for exmaple. Playing as 4 we never really did it too much but it was cool that it was an option.    Another aspect we didn't play but I assume is an option is PVPVE, similar ro Rust or Conan. I am never really into that sort of gameplay, however having concurrent colonies indepdently progressing in the world sounds cool.    In some co op survival games sometiems I feel like I am getting left behind or I don't have a "role", like everyone else is doing everything necessary already and I don't feel very useful. In this game I felt useful all the way through and like I always had a part to play.    Definitely one of the better, polished, survival/colony sims out there. It's especially nice because colony sims aren't usually MP, however if you are playing the game purely for coloniy sims aspect you are better off elsewhere.

Necesse is a standout indie gem that blends survival, exploration, and colony management into a surprisingly deep and rewarding experience. Its charming top-down pixel art may look simple at first glance, but it supports an impressively rich world full of biomes, secrets, and progression systems that constantly encourage exploration. The procedural islands give each playthrough a fresh feel, while the smooth performance and clear visuals make even busy base-building sessions easy to manage.    One of Necesse’s greatest strengths is how it balances player freedom with structure. You can play it as a solo survival adventure, a relaxed building and farming game, or a more strategic colony-management experience with settlers who work, craft, and defend your base. The automation and task systems feel intuitive rather than overwhelming, allowing your settlement to grow organically as you progress.    Combat and progression are equally satisfying. Boss fights are well-designed, distinct, and rewarding, providing clear milestones without feeling grindy. The gear, skills, and crafting systems offer meaningful choices that support different playstyles, from combat-focused exploration to efficient base optimization.    What truly elevates Necesse is its ongoing development and strong modding support. Regular updates add content, polish, and quality-of-life improvements, while the modding community expands the game’s potential even further. Combined with multiplayer support, Necesse becomes a game that’s easy to recommend to both casual players and hardcore fans of sandbox and survival games.    Overall, Necesse delivers depth, charm, and replayability far beyond what its price and presentation might suggest. It’s a thoughtfully designed game that respects the player’s time, rewards creativity, and continues to grow in all the right ways.

Bullet Hell + Settlement builder + Terraria = Necesse? This game has it all. One key thing this game does very well is its end game. It has a brilliant system that really challenges you and keeps you interested. It also has a huge variety of weapons and armors. The settler systems that allow you to gear them, bring them into battle, or have them go explore mine and fish is really unique and helpful.   My personal favorite thing about the game is the inventory system. You can assign dedicated storage to your town and can assign what goes in each storage container. You can name and change the appearance of the containers. Your settlers are automated to sort your inventory so I can just toss all my inventory into a dump chest and they will all flock and organize them into correct storage containers which is amazing. I'd say thats my biggest gripe of all these types of game and they finally found a fix for it.   I can see this game getting many updates in the future and becoming a labor of love. So I'd buy now, beat it, and then come back a year later probably to loads of new content. Enjoy.

The infinite world gen is one of the most interesting unique features, but it just ends up creating a big empty world. The settlement system is the best feature that sets it apart. The progression lacks the nuance of either of those games, for one thing the workstations/anvils reveal all the recipes you can make with them removing most of the sense of discovery that a first time player would have had otherwise.     Overall if you like Terraria or Core Keeper then i would recommend checking Necesse out, Just keep in mind that it's not currently at the same level as those two games, so temper your expectations. I'm hopeful that one day it will get the much needed polish and content.

How best to describe this game ... Probably close to "What if Dwarf Fortress and Rimworld were RPGs as well as colony simulators?"  Would be a good way to describe what this game is trying to do.  Starting and running a colony is completely optional, you can do everything yourself without missing anything important, which is good.  But the benefits of starting a town/colony are that you can automate everything: crops, animals, crafting, cooking, you can set recipes and stock limits, organize the chests for what goes where ... it's all very in depth and once set up frees up plenty more free time for exploration and combat.    Definitely a game that is more fun with friends than solo, but easily quite enjoyable solo too.    My main complaint is world generation, where there doesn't seem to be any large bodies of water breaking up the map aside from decently sized lakes.  So everything kinda blends together like a land of rivers and lakes.    That said, I'd give it a solid 8/10.  More fun with friends, but definitely with plenty of content for an enjoyable solo run

TLDR: game could be incredible but it's let down by unintuitive controller support - only buy if you plan on playing KBM  I want to love this game but the controller support is so unintuitive it hurts. I have even come back since getting a Steam Controller and the official layout, while better than a basic controller, still is super unintuitive.   Having the right trackpad be a mouse cursor is great in theory until you actually play the game. When using the trackpad and hovering over an item, you can't just use the back button to quick move the item, you have to hover over the item, use the dpad so it re-registers it's a controller and then use the back button. Similar to interacting with objects / using tools - you can use your trackpad cursor but then have to use the right stick to basically tell the game you're back to controller before you can interact with anything  Hope this is something the devs can fine tune but until I see a patch with updated controller support, I'm gonna have to give this a miss & leave this review as a warning that, even though it says full controller support, it's not great in my opinion
