This game was fantastic!!!!! Until this update. The whole shark fin controversy feels misguided. If we're worried about sensitivities, then animals should be removed entirely.   Think about it...we can stripmine the entire world, wipe out ecosystems, and obliterate villages in necesse, but suddenly, virtual sharks are where we draw the line?  Come on!! And let’s not pretend real world morality lines up here.  Villagers get slaughtered, homes looted no outcry there..?  Because "it's just a game." Meanwhile, actual slaughterhouses process thousands of cattle daily, sometimes inefficiently, sometimes cruelly.  It happens. I’m not endorsing it, but if we’re policing pixels, why stop at sharks? Should we remove every non-hostile NPC and animal? Either commit to the bit or don’t.    Shark finning? Terrible. But this is fiction. If we’re reshaping games based on selective outrage, where does it end?    Honestly, it’s frustrating. I’d respect the devs more if they stood by their original vision instead of backtracking with vague justifications.    I agree with the post below, but the discussion was locked.  So I'm dropping my thoughts here instead. That’s my two cents.    "Originally posted by Fair: @agedwarlock There was literally no "art" going into if it is scales or fins. Either work for the purposes of this art you mention. A designer of mine added it and I reviewed it without knowing the connotations. Nothing has changed in how I come up with new content, this was just a small mistake that I wanted to rectify. As I mentioned earlier, if it had just been scales from the start, no one, including you wouldn't have mentioned it at all. With fins a some people got upset, and I want to avoid that. I'm not sure why you're so mad about me not wanting to anger people? Oh really? Then how about we remove all of the cooked meat out of respect for the vegetarians and vegans who love to play this game? Even though shark fin soup is completely optional, their feelings might get hurt! We have to do the same for the meat in Necesse!  This was a dumb "Fix", No matter what you choose, someone will get offended, if they didn't want to hurt sharks? Then all they have to do is not kill the sharks. No reminder of the problem, no issue in the first place.  As if renaming it will help any issues at hand, no matter what its gonna be labeled as animal cruelty. This is a 2D game where you will go out of your way to kill lots of wildlife."   While I understand the intention behind changing shark-related items, I don't believe it will resolve the core issue, as people will still whine and complain regardless.    It’s clear that many players prefer the game in its original state, and it’s disheartening to see changes made in response to a vocal minority (whiny people). At this point, the only options seem to be either reverting to the original version or removing wildlife entirely—since, realistically, pleasing everyone isn’t possible.    I want to acknowledge the team’s hard work and the enjoyment the game provided before the removal of shark fin soup. However, yielding to pressure and eliminating content that many of us enjoyed has ultimately led me to move on to other games.    Thanks for everything up until now.

After an hour and a half as far as I can tell all you do is walk around, pick things up, and try to craft with them and that's basically the game. While there is combat, it's just holding down the mouse button with no particular skill or challenge. Food is easy to come by. Temperature is not a concern. Sleep is not a concern. Healing is easy and there are no injuries. No stats, just better gear. Equipment is just laying around in boxes all over the place. No goal other than what you set yourself. If you die you just respawn.  It's like Rimworld if you took away everything that makes Rimworld challenging and fun.  The UI sucks too. For example, what you can build is lists of icons in random order and not searchable consistently.  The tutorial is bare-bones, I wouldn't have known what was I doing except that I played Rimworld and recognized the mechanics. For example, you can get settlers but it's never explained that they can work for you, much less how to get them to work.

I keep buying these games hoping they will be good and I should know better by now. The combat in this game is outclassed by Gauntlet on NES. The progression system is the same loop you have played before. The UI makes no attempts to be usable. The characters dialog seems like it was written as placeholders and never updated. The art style is passible at best.  My test for these games is whether I want to respawn after I die and get my stuff, or just uninstall. After about 90 minutes I couldn’t think of a single thing in the game that I wanted to experience more of, and chose the latter.  Unless you just want to do the same exact same thing you have been doing for 10 years in Terraria, pass on this one.

Players can recruit npc to grind for your resources but they refuse work if you do not provide humane living quarters for them - bed, table, light, and access to food; they hate sharing rooms.   At night time, zombies attack you - you can also be invaded by other npcs which break and steal your resources.  Overall, Necesse is an endless cycle of gather resources (like all survival games); it does help with the itch of wanting to farm for rocks.  After 60 plus hours, I am ready for a new game - its kinda boring.

Updated based on Dev Response. Original review is below.  Fair,  It was Sage and Grit. The specific situation that got bad was a negative feedback loop where getting killed by them required me to go through a roughly 10 minute process to get back to the point where I could challenge them at a semi-optimal level, only for the insane speed that they move at and the multi-hit behavior to make the fight feel comically unfair. Also, because I was bleeding resources in every fight, I ran out fairly quickly and that 10 minute cycle turned into an hour, at which point I gave up. Like I said in the base review, I'm sure I could beat it eventually, it just felt horrible to fall backwards every time I lost.  It also doesn't help at all that Sage and Grit require you to track two snake enemies that can turn faster than you can move outside of quick dodge effects and once you kill one of them, the other goes mach 3 and becomes significantly harder to dodge. If I wanted that, I'd go boot Ikaruga or something similar as those games don't have the resource limitations that Necesse has (which are a good thing outside of this specific context).  I do want to clarify that prior to that point, I was having a blast and the difficulty scaling felt right. It's not that the game got harder, I understand that the intent was for Necesse to scale in difficulty as it goes. That was clear from the start. It's that Sage and Grit were almost like running into a wall and the things to beat it (Fully equipped guards being the biggest problem as the necessity of them was not clear at all until that fight) required an exponential time sink to figure out and do. I know exactly how to fix the problem, I just don't want to spend 20+ hours doing something that the game probably assumed I was doing the whole time, particularly when grinding specifically for the resources to make the needed gear just isn't overly fun.   One major friction point for me is the game design choice to have guards go home after they have been dismissed or killed. Maybe it would be better to have them stay in your party, and just not do anything aggressive if you don't have food, or potentially have an actual party screen that shows all your guards and the moment you put food in it, they just teleport to you? I found that it was such a pain to try and keep them following me throughout the game that while I had geared them up somewhat, I only had like 3 guards by that point as I hadn't needed them prior to Sage and Grit and it took so much time to get them all organized, only for them to drain food and just leave once it was gone. I think I would have valued them more prior to that point if they had been attached to me by the hip and I had constantly felt the need to arm them correctly. I also think the summon build I was going for might have hidden the necessity of well built guards for a long time.  Increasing the spawn rate of resources would also alleviate this considerably, and while I know the miner exists, the fact that I constantly have to go find him to send him off on missions doesn't help. Maybe making the miner go find things passively like some of the other settlers might be better.  One last thing, I did find those difficulty settings and I think I clicked through them quickly on the first go. I was on classic. It might not be the worst idea to just move classic over one, rename it Classic (Fast), and make a strong point in the description that the game will demand very fast reflexes further down the line. Another difficulty setting that starts the same way and just doesn't scale quite as hard into the endgame speed-wise would also be appreciated, maybe a Classic (Slow), where the numbers are the same but the late game enemies are at half speed. The raw numbers were something I can beat with ease and my DPS was never the issue in that fight, nor was taking a single hit, I could technically heal through that and my summons alone had Sage and Grit at half health consistently. I just could not survive the speed increase once one of them died. I physically don't have a fast enough reaction time to consistently avoid getting multi-hit in that situation. For context, my most played game is War Thunder, where jets move at Mach 1-2 and stuff occasionally happens in milliseconds. If I'm struggling with the speed things are moving, that's probably a sign that the enemies are too fast.  I will return to Necesse later on and give it another shot, I'm currently playing a few other games and my bandwidth is limited. Thanks for the reply!   --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Original Review: ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  I was so close to whole heartedly recommending this game, and then I ran into a late game boss, found a difficulty spike via a bad game design choice that is not appropriate at all for this kind of game, saw that there were ways to cheese it but also that things only got worse from there and gave up to preserve my blood pressure. (Clarifying, the boss in question is the infamous one that shows up in the Deep Desert Caves.)  Necesse is a great game for a lot of playtime, but I can't recommend it when it goes two-faced at the worst possible moment. Difficulty in real time games isn't a problem for me (I got very far into Monster Hunter World a while back and Titanfall 2 multiplayer was my bread and butter for years), but in a game like this, demanding a perfect build and pixel perfect movement in a game that was downright cozy up to that point is unacceptable. I bought this for the fun rimworld/minecraft style overworld and boss fights up to that point that are well thought out and give you the freedom to beat them how you like. I did not buy this game to run into a boss that punishes you for playing the game any other way than the exact way required to beat it.  (And before anybody asks, I was using a Rifle+Summon build, not melee, and I had killed every prior boss in a single try. I'm also 100% certain that I could eventually figure the current boss out but that's not why I was playing Necesse in the first place.)  Worth the buy if wildly on sale or if you can stomach the gut punch that comes later. Honestly, if you are a masochist or have been pavlov'd by Elden Ring, you will probably like the change in direction that I'm talking about. Otherwise stay away from Necesse. If the dev sees this, I would sincerely recommend backing off the speed that some of the later bosses move at and removing the multihit behavior as both clash wildly with the feel of the game otherwise, and if these elements are things that you think need to be in the game, add difficulty settings so that the people who want them can have them and those of us who play this game to relax don't run into this stuff.

This is just a very boring terraria clone with a tacked-on, pointless colony management element.  The progression loop is pretty straight forward, you go mining for equipment in one biome, fight the biome-specific boss, and move on the next biome where you do the exact same thing. The problem is that it's the only thing you'll be doing for the whole game. The different biomes are also extremely similar to each other with the cave layouts being the same in all of them and the enemies being mostly reskins of each other with very minor differences like shooting a single projectile or flying through walls.  What makes this even worse is that the starting NPC hand-holds you every step of the way, telling you exactly where you have to go next after every boss.  The weapons are also very boring. Not only are they thematically vey bland, without any of the creativity that terraria weapons had, but their attacks are generally very basic as well. there are a few interesting ones like a summoner bow that summons temporary zombie minions on enemy hits or a magic flask you throw that has random effects (including detrimental ones), but these are few and far between.   You also have your settlement which sounds interesting on paper, but in reality it gives you no real reason to engage with it in any meaningful way. You can throw all your villagers in 5x5 boxes and feed them nothing but raw vegetables, but as long as you fill their room with enough random crap and have more than one food item for them to eat they'll be satisfied enough.  This game has a billion different crops and cooking ingredients but you don't even have to engage with any of that because the happiness system is so shallow that it doesn't really give you that much of a benefit.  The only real use for the villagers is to give them your gear and take them on expeditions with you.  There is also a post-game in the form of incursions, but honestly they all just seem kind of repetitive and grindy so I don't really feel like doing them. I know that means I technically didn't "Beat the game" but I don't really see the point if I don't enjoy them.

The game is a top-down Terraia-Like, and that itself is fine, however for a heavily combat-focused game the bosses are really bad. In almost all cases the boss battles are bullet sponges that boil down to one of the following types of experience...  1. Cagey humanoid (or other) which zooms around the perimeter of the screen, often leaving your view entirely, while lobbing volleys of explosives or other wide attacks at you.  2. Bullet hell bosses, but at least those have attack patterns.  3. Worm-like bosses that move around at high speed before charging at you from offscreen to chunk your healthbar.  More details...  The first type moves so fast that most projectiles can't keep up with them, generally playing chicken at the edge of your view while lobbing whatever area attack or explosive that fits it's flavour. This makes melee near useless, most magic attacks (besides laser-type) too slow to reliably hit, and still just really annoying with ranged weapons. These bosses will often charge from off screen to smack you at random, with no set attack pattern but just homing in on you, creating a frustrating situation where you struggle to apply consistent damage while they shoot at you as though you were a fish in a barrel.  Type two, the bullet hell style bosses, generally just run or teleport around while spamming various attack patterns. At least with these you have a more standard experience and can learn to avoid the attacks, but some seem poorly telegraphed. For example there is a dark wizard that will zoom around and use either a laser beam that you must be near to avoid, or use attacks which do the opposite, being near unavoidable at close range. There isn't really a telegraph for which will be used. This just makes fights feel like uncomfortable DPS checks instead of pattern learning.  Type three is the worst. Big tanky worms or dragons that all use variations of the same AI, they will alternate between charging at you from offscreen, coralling you, using energy attacks, and just flying around trying to hit you. For example there is a massive centipede that will wrap around you like a game of snake while going nearly invulnerable, then charge away before wrapping around and zooming in at you vertically from offscreen. There is no telegraph, the thing is extremely fast, it homes in on you, and you are still wrapped up in the center of it's death coil when it hits. There's not really a way to avoid the hit, which will chunk your HP even in heavy armor sets. Even with move speed you cant sidestep it easily, and dodging just makes you hit the coil. Blocking doesn't work because it's outrageously long body lingers in place for 10 sec, in fact it will generally make a second pass at you before the coil untangles. These are absolutely the most frustrating bosses to fight, and this pattern is reused over and over.   You can bring your entire village with you to help in the fight, but that's either no fun, or in the case of the giant centipede and other long wormy things, they cover the entire screen in noodle, which your villagers will die to very quickly.  The main problem is that there is no telegraphed pattern, bosses just hover out of view before rushing in and covering your entire screen with damage areas. In most cases even if you do dodge/sidestep an attack, the bosses have so much aim correction that they will still manage to twist and hit you before doing an offscreen u-turn and making another pass.  Right, and I forgot to mention the iframes are minimal, so in the case of the annoying worm-like bosses, you'll still be stuck in the middle of their bodies after being hit, usually getting hit 2-3 times whenever this happens and losing over half your HP.  Fun general gameplay, very poor boss design. I enjoyed it until it turned into nothing but worm-boss hunting late game. If you had a few friends maybe their attention would be split, but it's a frustrating experience playing alone. The centipede just sits on you for example, since it can freely lasso you then run you over like a freight train from offscreen after your stuck in the center (Once there, there is no escaping without taking damage). Villagers cannot even help here as the attack (snake body) is persistent and covers the screen, they all die very quickly.  -  mmm, one more example of bad boss design to make the point. There this big spider that alternates between charging at you and coving large parts of the conflic area with persistent aoe puddles (eventually nowhere is safe to walk). The problem is when it's not doing that it's backing away from you in very skittery ways, avoiding you. This has several effects...  1. Melee is useless 2. Slower to avg projectile weapons miss a lot since it skitters around the edge of the screen. 3. It feels frustrating because it acts chicken then flips around with no wind up, spews at you or charges straight at you, then immedietely plays chicken again. 4. Often it ends up offscreen and charging at you from out of view (a common theme). 5. While skittering away from you it invariably ends up inside a wall because it can do that, and all your projectiles hit the wall instead of the boss. It can still bombard you though, the spiders attacks don't care about walls. (FRUSTRATING!)

Necesse is one of those games that looks perfect on paper—Top-down Terraria with settlement management? Sign me up. But in reality, it’s just a test of how much punishment you can tolerate.  I am genuinely annoyed by the boss design. It doesn't feel like a skill check; it feels cheap. The bosses have attacks that feel unavoidable or just straight-up unfair, spamming projectiles until you die not because you messed up, but because the game decided you needed to die. There is no satisfaction in beating them, just relief that it’s over.  And the rest of the game? It is way too grindy.  You spend hours hitting rocks and clearing the same caves just to make a tiny bit of progress. The loop of "explore, gather, upgrade" is usually fun in this genre, but here it feels like a second job. You are constantly gated by resources that take forever to find, and the payoff just isn't worth the time investment.  Good concept, but the unfair bosses and tedious grind ruin the experience.

[h1]Terraria, Upgraded and Downgraded[/h1]  First things first: 85% of the bafflingly named Necesse (I have no clue how to pronounce it either) is an utterly shameless Terraria clone.  * There are four combat styles: Melee, Ranged, Magic, and Summoning. Just like Terraria.  * Once you've cleared all the bosses in the starter biomes, you get access to advanced "Deep" versions of the biomes which crank up the difficulty and add all-new materials, weapons, and armor. This mode is gated by a boss (except this time it's not a horrific Wall of Flesh, it's a Pirate). Just like Terraria.  * You'll farm biomes beyond their usefulness in search of treasure rooms that contain rare drops. Just like Terraria.  * You'll build a town in order to attract NPCs who offer various services. Just like Terraria.  * You'll need to beat back semi-regular town invasions. And though there are traps you can collect while exploring which can sort of be automated to help defend, they're not strong enough to actually be meaningful. Just like Terraria.  And so on. There's even a Dungeon biome that's decorated in a way essentially identical to Terraria's Dungeon. It's so similar that I fully expected to kill an Old Man that turns into Skeletron to gain access.  As for the remainder:   * 5% of is a mediocre colony sim (think Rimworld or Oxygen Not Included). They'll plant and harvest crops, chop trees, raise animals, and all that extra stuff people usually end up ignoring in survival games. Properly configure your storage containers and they'll even organize your stock of items for you. You can even set them up to automatically craft stuff if you want (though beyond the food they require and automatically smelting bars, there isn't much point).  * 5% are gameplay mods to Terraria (Melee builds up an HP buffer called "Resilience" by attacking, NPCs can be paid to automatically gather materials for you, NPCs can be given armor, weapons, food, and potions, and accompany you as an adventuring party).  * The last 5% is the functionally endless endgame activity, [b]Incursions[/b]. These are Maps from Path of Exile. They require "Incursion Tablets" (aka Rift Stones or Maps) to open, which drop in Incursions. Tablets of course have random mutators on them that make the Incursion harder.    Open an Incursion and the game generates a tiny biome based on the tablet you plug in. You're assigned a simple mission like mining x ores or killing y enemies, and when you finish that you get to fight one of the bosses.  Win and you get endgame currencies, tablets, and possibly even more.  The endgame currencies are used to make and upgrade endgame weapons and armor, endgame potions, and the meta-progression skill tree for Incursions that's basically like the Atlas from Path of Exile.  If Incursions sound interesting, You should know you don't get access to them until you've beaten the Terraria clone's final boss. Expect that to take 20-25 hours on a first playthrough.  [h1]Baby Come Back[/h1]  Ultimately I found myself wishing I'd just played Terraria again instead. Incursions were an interesting curiosity, but by no means are they worth the ticket price of a 20-25 hour playthrough of a mediocre version of Terraria.   Save your money - and more importantly, your time - and just replay Terraria instead.

Was fun for a while but there is a elephant in the room they don't really address. And that is the adventure party system. It is really a core element of the game. You can maybe traverse the dungeons solo, but you really need a party to fight bosses, defend your base, etc.  You will end up repeating the same thing over again. You will gather a bunch of party members, attempt to equip them all which is difficult because they move around. You can have them pause, but AFAIK there is no way to just cycle through the current members to look at each one's gear one by one.  So you gather your party then eventually you will die. And you lost all your party members. To head back out you have to go chase them down in your base then add them again.  At least they added some kind of awkward group button, but it's still not that great. It's baffling to me while the whole party thing is a big dynamic of the game they mostly ignore essential game play control for them. Doesn't make sense this neglect, it's like they are not even addressing it; frustrating,  I had to give this game up and I wouldn't recommend it in it's current state.  I started playing "Core Keeper" instead and it is a superior game for the same genre. More polished, more character, etc., play Core Keeper instead of this game. At least until they have actually addressed a key element of this game, control and QOL for the adventure party system.

The game itself is… fine. Not bad, not great. Just okay.  What really killed it for me was the grind and the RNG. Both are excessive and slowly drain any fun you might be having. This is a VERY soft "do not recommend", in that I don't think the game is horrible or bad but I just... wouldn't recommend it. The first ten hours or so of the game just feels like a generic terraria-clone, but locked behind the psudeo final boss and presented as optional post game content are incursions and weapon tiers. This part of the game is actually pretty interesting and fun; I consider it to be the REAL game! And it's a HUGE shame you have to slog through the first half of the game to get to that point. According to the steam achievements, only about 13% of players unlock that part of the game, only 8% ever TRY the post game, and only **1%** beat the ACTUAL final boss, through no fault of their own. That's really really bad! I don't blame anyone who dropped the game before they got to that point, because there is never even any indication that there is anything worth doing post game! To loosely quote a friend: "The game is only fun once it stops copying terraria." If the game had ONLY been the post game content, and expanded on that post game content, I would find this to be a game worth recommending. But I just can't bring myself to recommend a game where what I consider to be the fun part only starts many hours in (and also just doesn't last very long).

I have put 1,600+ hours into Rimworld and 1,700+ hours into Terraria. I see incredibly obvious, almost actionable copying of the dynamics of both in this game, which on the surface might seem to be a natural recipe for something great, but this is a pale and unsatisfying imitation of either without any real understanding of what makes both prior games endlessly replayable. The NPC system is poor, the progression is extremely feathery, insubstantial and easy to leapfrog, the gathering too generous, the settlement system shoddily implemented without the depth and granular detail of Rimworld and the 2D combat as arcadey as Terraria but just being endless samey hordes of a handful of separate creatures per biome without any of the strategy needed in exploring the original game's depths, even Stardew Valley's cave combat tops this for variety and skill.    Now, things I did like? The rare flashes of actual originality, like the frozen enemies in the snow biome that slowly thaw and give you a second to either attack or flee from the crowd. Now it's possible this is only new to me and ripped off from another game I haven't played, but I enjoyed it. The puzzle dungeons are a nice change...but I kept running into the exact same type, with the light up path, so suffice it to say it won't even be giving NES-era Legend of Zelda a run for its money in that regard. The combat patterns for the bosses, at least the first five or six, are well done and seem to be the only cases in the game where you have to actually think out how you want to fight. The bosses however suffer greatly from not having a continuing theme like Terraria of being part of a greater menace you're working towards, if there's any kind of lore at all in this to make it seem like a richer universe, I haven't found it.    At any rate, I got a good sixty hours during which I felt compelled to keep playing to see how it would develop for the better, aided by the fact everything seemed so incredibly familiar given my experience with other games, but I had to regretfully conclude that there's just no reason to continue when the games it so sorely strives to emulate are just vastly superior. Now the caveat, if you've never played either of the games I've mentioned and you want a homey little top-down open-world settlement builder/roguelike....try it! You might have a lot more fun, especially if it's fresh and new.    You clearly tried to make a fun game, Necesse, so points for effort. But can you at least explain why you felt the need to rip off Terraria so blatantly that you didn't even bother RENAMING THE POTIONS?
It's just not fun, bosses are way overtuned and grindy, oneshot mechanics are not it.

I wondered a little whether or not I should give this one a thumbs up or thumbs down, then realized that the fact I was asking myself that meant it wasn't good enough for a thumbs up.  I know there are many games that overstay their welcome in the early access world. Necesse, however, could've benefited from extra updates before calling itself a full release.  This game suffers from a lack of incentives and has little to no reason to make you want to keep going. It's like it has all the good ingredients, but doesn't know what to do with them. You can collect a whole bunch of items, but it seems like there's a huge lack of purpose in the long run.  Gameplay-wise it's very similar to Terraria but with a Stardew Valley-like view. It's simple and effective. There's many weapons to fight with, which is pretty cool. The combat is like any game of that genre - responsive but limited. Enemies come in various forms and have different attacks which is cool. Some of them are clearly inspired from past games ( ex: the Minecraft zombie ).  There's a decent amount of tools - classic tools you see in similar games. It can be grindy if you don't pay attention to the main mechanic of the game which is building settlements.  Because what makes this game stand out is the mechanic of having NPCs populate areas you claim and building settlements, making them work like in a colony game similar to Rimworld, but it lacks depth. The characters lack personality for multiple reasons and feel more like drones than anything else. For starters, they are not introduced properly - they just arrive at your village one at a time, giving you the opportunity to recruit them. Each should have a little backstory with a quest to make them memorable. Then, once they are given a home and jobs, they never have interactions with their environment besides what you told them to do. I think that Necesse should go deeper with that mechanic and introduce randomly generated stories, create relationships and rivalries between NPCs, things that the player would seldom have to intervene in and settle. They should have some kind of history and purpose greater than being tools to help us getting more items faster.  Visually, it's a very charming, colorful game. I like the icons for the items, the infoboxes, etc. The UI is alright. The music is okay but gets on your nerves quickly due to a lack of songs. There's an issue with music changing frequently due to them being associated with biomes and biomes being very small, so when you're travelling, you constantly change songs back and forth. I'd suggest that they either make their biomes way bigger or stick to random songs no matter the biome.  I'm sure I'll keep playing this game some more in the future, and I do enjoy parts of it, but it's just not enough at the moment. I'm less than 10 hours in and I felt that drain that you feel in survival crafters when you get tired of the grind, which is pretty bad news if you ask me. It's not that it's hard to find the materials, it's just that it's not fun to collect them. There should be something, like a tribute of X items to give after a specific time, or quest-related travels that force you to break your routine. Right now, there's only enemy raids which in reality aren't super fun, because there is no character progression besides the items you collect and upgrade. There is no leveling system and no perk or talent system besides buffs that come from wearables and potions.  I think this game has everything it needs to become great. It has a very stable ground to work on. Now it just needs to build on that and expand some more. Then, I'll very likely change my review.

It’s a Terraria-like game that somehow manages to miss even the basic depth that Terraria offers. It borrows heavily - mechanics, items, visual style - yet brings almost nothing new to the table.    Settlements sound promising at first… for about an hour. Then you realize the NPCs are just reskinned Terraria townsfolk with shallow automation: they farm, chop trees, herd animals - but that’s it. Sure, some are mildly useful (the miner, for instance, can gather mid-to-late-game ores), but that leads right into the next problem: progression is painfully vertical.    Early-game items and ores quickly become obsolete. Why keep a miner around when you can dive into a mine and gather tier-X resources in ten minutes - only to never need them again once you hit the next tier?    Bosses suffer from the same lack of imagination. Most are just fast-moving blobs spitting particle effects at you. Only a handful feel distinct or memorable. Weapon variety? Barely there - especially for the summoner class, whose gear feels repetitive and uninspired.    And then there’s the grind. Oh boy, the grind. Want a “unique” item, like the reward for the bear quest in the first cave biome? Be ready to mine for an hour. Just mine. No variation, no mechanics, no fun - just mind-numbing repetition.    In short: it copies Terraria’s weakest points, adds its own flaws on top, and offers nothing fresh or exciting in return. If you’re looking for a true Terraria successor, this isn’t it.  UPD: Explorer makes grind less, because he can find some unique items in caves.

The town building and exploration in this game are great, especially now that it all exists in one big map instead of separate ones. However, the combat and progression are the weakest part of this game for me by a significant margin.     You will fight stronger and stronger enemies, and all you get are small percentage increases to some stats that make you just on par with them, without ever giving you new, cool abilities. Every single stage of progression is identical except some different sprites and numbers.    You go into a biome  You fight that biomes infinite horde of the same couple enemies  You mine that biomes singular unique ore  You make that biomes recolored armor and weapons  You fight the biomes singular unique boss  Repeat until the end of the game.    There is close to zero differentiation between the early game and the late game.  The only part of the game I found to be meaningfully changed was the options for what I could build my house from, and what food I could make.  This loop would be fine for the first 4 biomes, but once I got into the ~Deep Biomes~ I realized just how little new there was to experience.  I have yet to get my mining speed over +30% of its baseline, and mind you every single biomes stone is mined at the exact same rate. I have yet to get my movement speed to be even remotely fast enough for where I want to go, even on minecarts. I have yet to be able to kill the very first enemies of the game in less than 3 hits. I do not feel any stronger after all these hours.    Couldn't figure out where else to put this, so I'm just slapping this at the end. In most games, a unique and challenging boss might come with 2 clearly separated phases, or 3 if you're REALLY going off the wall. In this game, every single boss gains access to more and better attacks as the fight goes on, with no clear indication of when they are getting them or why. I swear some bosses have upwards of 5 attacks that can only happen between two invisible health thresholds, not that I have actually counted though. Most are made for a character faster than I have the capacity to be at that point.    If you want to get the game, I'm sure you could have some fun with it. But the alternatives are better. I was hoping 1.0 would make this game better, but all it did was make me pay money to continue playing what is ostensibly the same product.

Like many of the other similar reviews, I feel the game in a very odd style of choices - missing that special flavor, the magic or pizzazz. Jack of all trades, master of none type feeling ended up with it feeling strangely shallow, despite all the things it attemps to do.   At about 5 hours of play time, I had my smallish colony running more or less happily with no actual need to ever touch it again - exploration topside led only to endless "very samey" biomes and NPC settlements. By the time I saw a few of them - I had seen them all. Loot was very underwhelming, Despite running around in a gold armor set and sporting an enchanted weapon (and guns) - combat felt rather underwhelming and had that odd underdog flavor. Cave or underworld had a very similar vibe, except that it is  annoyingly dark and sported endlessly spawning enemies. After beating the first boss, Im not sure Im willing to give it anymore time...  Luckily after falling for the hype, I managed to get from a sale - and would recommend you do the same if you absolutely must.

Have to disagree with the majority on this one - I didn't have a lot of fun with this one.  I heard this game described as a combination of Terraria, Minecraft, and Rimworld, which I think is quite accurate, but not in a good way - in trying to do many different things, it makes each of them feel shallow.  It has the unsatisfying gameplay loop of Terraria (kill boss -> get loot -> use loot to kill next boss -> get more loot, repeat), but lacks its charm.  It has procedurally generated worlds, like Minecraft, but Minecraft made that world interesting by allowing the player to interact with and reshape it in a satisfying manner, which this game lacks.  It has RimWorld's graphics and the tedium of colony management, but not the rewarding diversity of characters - in this game, you can interact with an infinite number of procedurally generated NPCs and towns, and not a single one of them will be significant or memorable in any way.  The friend I played with seemed to enjoy the game (he said he found the colony management relaxing), but I could not for the life of me figure out what I wanted to do - there were many things I could do, but none of them were actually enjoyable.

I guess there's a different set of expectations here for what makes a "1.0 ready" game.  Despite the fact that this game has been in development for a long time, it remains very unpolished. The concepts are great, with village management and exploration, there's not quite any other games I can compare it to in terms of what it provides.  However, that does not excuse what I would consider very poor execution.  For art style: This is a very nice style, a more pixelated look can make things feel nice and encourages the creativity of the artist, but the textures in this scenario are just awful. My eyes glazed over, looking at the crafting menu and seeing a massive blob of "things" that make it profoundly difficult to quickly find what you're trying to craft at a glance. I found myself using the search bar to find things, which is a nice add, but that doesn't mean it should be a crutch for a system where better artistic style would make it easier to point out what you're trying to craft. Along with that, something feels odd with the aliasing, as it feels like I am looking at everything with glasses off. That alone gives the game a very unpolished feel.  For gameplay: I'm willing to give this a "pass", not that it means its' good. The villager automation is cool and reminds me of RimWorld, but lacks a level of depth that would make it comparable. The movement is janky and it feels like you're floating wherever you walk, not to mention that you move very slow, even when you having the boots that allow you to run equipped.  Right now, that's all I have to say about this game. I didn't get very far, because for some reason the game crashes every time I tabbed out to take notes. Maybe another time I'll pick this back up, but from the past 2 tries I've given it, I was not impressed. Beware if you're looking to buy this game, I'm sure the content is there, but you must lower your standards.

Necesse is missing something in the sauce. It’s fine. It’s the white noise of coop terraria/minecraft-likes. It avoids making any huge stumbles and won't piss you off, but fails to really elevate itself meaningfully. What you do in the first hour is more or less what you’ll be doing by the 30th. For $10 I’d recommend it if you’re bored. Anything more and you might wonder why you bought something so bland.  The npc sim that is one of the big selling points is… *fine*. It’s nice and powerful, sure, but why? Why does it exist? *Oopsie, just because*! They needed to either provide some game design reasons for it to exist (e.g., factory-style; having to actually give giant clumps of resources to your king or something) or to make the npc sim way more personable, like Dragon Quest Builders 2, where the npcs are way more greedy. It’s not even like Terraria where you’re trying to figure out how to get npcs to move in or what makes them happy — they show up like an unceasing assembly line to your base from the jump.  In Necesse, you can set up 25 3x3 prisons, give the npcs only a diet of raw vegetables, make them work all day long and they’ll be like 95% happy. You’ll probably have this figured out in the first few hours. Whereas in DQB2, the npcs want taverns. They want toilets. They want baths. They want a pool. They want a sauna. They want a castle. They come to you with demands. They want all sorts of things — that you can make just as well in Necesse, but there’s no reason to. Eventually it becomes white noise on a tv screen that you completely ignore.  Combat is fine. There’s lots of bosses. There’s a decent amount of weapon variety, but the AI is braindead and lacks any ability to pressure you meaningfully so it ends up not mattering.  Like most games mimicking Terraria, Necesse totally fails to reinvent itself after the first act. Trinkets and mounts and weapons are just not impactful enough. You don’t transform from the lowly trudging settler into a racing, flying, unstoppable vestige of death. You’re just the same dude, with a little bit more particle vfx and higher numbers. Monsters similarly — they’re basically all the same walking towards you in clumps that all do the same things.  The art is ugly, even after this redesign. Biomes lack personality. Exploration is flat and boring, there needs to be more POIs and definitely more ore in the walls — it’s way too empty feeling. The “Deep Caves” which is the quasi-hardmode area is incredibly unfun to explore because of lava being everywhere. It needed to be limited to lava POIs rather than making every biome covered in annoying lava pools.  And that’s one of the pitfalls of the infinite generated world — you never get the sense that you’re pushing into some exciting new boundary, like in Terraria, or Core Keeper’s world generation 2.0 re-do. You just explore the same biomes over and over and pray you get some new POI. Even if you do it often doesn’t have anything terribly interesting or useful.  Enemies don’t even appear to drop anything useful either — all your main weapons and items are either going to be from ores, bosses, or POIs — so there’s no real point to killing anything.  Overall, the game is fine. How much more mid you want in your life is up to you.

For context. Played thru the game up to incursion 9 with a friend, was able to beat that without too much struggle, but could not bother to farm up the last couple thousand arcane dust to unlock the tree up to the final boss.  Aside from the idea of the settlement folks being able to automate the routine tasks around the base, this is just top down diet terraria, so many things are close to being satisfying, without ever getting there. Most of the spells and summons are very similar to like 3 types and lackluster in creativity outside visuals.   The settlement system is cool, but is severely lacking in quality of life for things like designating chests contents or setting quantities or conditions to do work, all those systems exist but are lacking in implementation and ease of use.  Almost none of the armor sets or trinkets do anything "cool", and the ones that do are lackluster in power level, some are literally detrimental to use (looking at you armor that blows up minions for less damage than the minion hits for)  Art was fine, didn't hit any bugs that I remember, all over that kind of stuff was fine, its well made, just poorly designed.  3/10 experience, it was a distraction that went from OK to meh rapidly as the content just repeated itself over and over with a new coat of paint. If I didn't play it with a friend I likely would have returned it before the two hour mark.

Do I recommnend this game? Sort of. Could it be improved? Absolutely. Is it fun? For a time...   I'm playing co-op with my friend and we're having quite a bit of fun doing it. Having said that, the mechanics/systems get boring pretty fast. Here are some of the negatives:  1- Progression: The gear progression is odd...  At the begining, you gain access to starter tools but quite quickly (and almost at the same time) you unlock copper, iron and gold which allows you to skip the progression of the in-between materials, launching from starter tools to.. well.. gold in no time. I found this disapointing and completely underwhelming. I would have loved a slower progression towards unlocking these better tools. This seems to have robbed me of any sense of achivement regarding said tools.  2- Biome progression: So far, the world appears to be nothing but the same biomes, stitched together on a wide, open map. I would have loved to see the world become tougher, the biomes changing as I walk away from my start location and run 3000m 5000m, 10,000, away, in a straight line, away from home. Alas, it appears that it's just the same biomes, at the same difficulty, over and over again, no mater how far I make it away from home. The same loot, the same monsters, the same biomes. Seems to me like there is very little reason to spread out and build in any other location other than my start location. The first level of dungeons appears to be the same; the same dungeon biomes, repeated ad nauseam, with the same loot, same monsters, same difficulty, no mater how far I travel.  3) Gear crafting: Once I found and went into the swamp dungeon, a quick run around allowed me enough material to build an entire armor set. It didn't take much effort; it didn't take very much time, it didn't take any planning. There was no 'working on' this gear progression; it simply happened once I found the right biome. Found the ore, processed it and voila; a brand new armor set with minimal effort. In another hour, I made 3 more, for my settlers.  As much as I enjoy playing this game with my friend, now that I know what I'm doing, I can't see myself playing it for a second playthorugh. It lacks a real sense of progression. It lacks depths. I didn't take long for me to get a 'same old, same old' feeling, when I left my settlement to go on an 'adventure'. Very quickly, any sense of adventure gets replaced with a sense of 'I've seen this 100 times already'. Once you know what you're doing, progression will be absurdly easy and would require very little effort / play time.   In short, it lacks a ense of biome progression. It lacks a sense of gear progression. It very quickly devolves into a feeling of 'same old' biomes, same old monsters, same old loot. There is very little to ZERO need to spread out and rebuild anywhere. There is no need to build a step up base. There is no need to explore anything other than the biomes that you can already access from the first hour or 2 of playing.   This game has some good, strong bones. Alas, the implemention seems... off...  After 24+hours of gameplay, the game seems very shallow. It lacks a solid progression system. The sense of adventure quickly wears thin and dissapears almost entirely. Perhaps I just got addicted to the likes of Valheim, where opening a new biome feels exciting; where exploring and maping new island makes me happy; even after 1000+ hours of gametime.   The Pros: - Settlement building was fun. Had a great time building our home, building settler homes, building farms, animal pens, roads, chest rooms and so on. The dev did a great job on this! Lots of decorations, building bocks etc.  - Love the 'cosmetic'  slots that allowed us to wear one armor set while looking like another. Kudos for letting us look how we WANT to look. My friend was super-happy to wear the frog head while I loved rocking purple robes, orange boots and a bone headpiece. Alas, 'metal' progression doesn't really exist so we didn't need to update our settler rooms. They don't seem to care if something is made from wood, copper or gold. We'd love to see a more in depth 'room happiness' mechanic. Of course, this means that the ease of which you can access iron, gold etc would need to be re-tooled.   - The ability to 'mod' your chacter using trinkets was amazing; From being able to build for mana-regen to extra armor. We loved the ability to tailor our enchants based on our build wants. We spent WAY too much gold re-rolling our enchants :)  but we see this as a positive because this meants that even 24+ hours in, gold still retained value for us.    ------------- Suggestions:  - Change the whole map system where you can hit tougher biomes when you travel far away. Give us a reason to build step-up bases; to link bases using mine cart rails. To fight out way through the map to get to other, tougher places. As it stands, there are zero reasons for us to move away from where we started.   - Need a 'settler leveling' system where we can assign points to our settlers. They can acquire XP from completing certain tasks. IE: Unlock cook recipes the more they cook. Unlock better mage enchants the more they enchant our stuff etc. This would breath new life and new choices into our settlers. Once programmed into their tasks, we had no need to even care that they existed. We have no emtional connection to them; there's no need for us to interact with them other than pay our mage for enchants.  - Would love a skill based leveling system where we become better at mining, farming and so on. Killing countless thousands of monsters, in a game where spawns just keeps comming, holds little to no value. There is no real reason to keep killing monsters. If anything, the goal is to drop 100,000 torches to monsters STOP spawning since mobs don't have any value other than being a general annoyance.   - Speaking of useless monsters: We'd love to see a "+1% damage to <x> after 100 kills, 1k kills, 10k kills, 25kills, 50k kills (or some such). At the very least, it would give us a small tangible benefit for slauthering mobs and add some goals for a small increase in damage to certain bosses. ;) (example: 10k purple mage kills gives me a 4% extra dam on the mage boos? Heck yeah! Bring on the farming slaughter!) I love such proverbial carrot danglings. They give me purpose, something to strive for.  --------------------------  Overall, it's fun but in its current state, I'd pass if I wasn't playing with a friend. For me, this game offers zero replayability. I'm of the opinion that this game needs some serious redesign from someone who has experience with progression systems.    We had fun poking around and learning about the gameplay systems. Once those were figured out, there's not enough depth to keep us here. For me, replayailty value is zero; which makes me sad. I truly wanted to love this game.

Practically a Terraria clone sprinkled with a "RimWorld/Dwarf Fortress"-style settlement management. The caveat is that is takes the worst from the both worlds.  I will start with the latter part. You can recruit/find various settlers and assign several different work orders to them like managing a forest, animal handling or farming. It's one to one from the RimWorld, with zones, work orders, priorities and bedroom assignments. And that's it. You have what basically is a bunch of one-dimensional slaves to haul and craft. Borrowed diet and grades of meals from RimWorld baffle me the most. This is in-game automation of menial tasks which become irrelevant and self-serving several hours into the game. Speaking of automation, all crafting orders have to be done manually. No strategic bird-view of RimWorld, where the whole settlement is at your fingertips. You can assign a storage or a workstation to the settlement, but still you need to haul your slow avatar to the workbench or anvil to make "do X thing Y times" orders.  And the former part. The similarities border on blatant copying. Where do I start? Characters and their inventories separate from the world, acting as a save files? Running around, breaking stuff to loot cave systems and different biomes? Equipment modifiers capped at exactly 4%? Tool progression through better ores and loot? Don't get me wrong, none of these are bad things in themselves, or a excuse to throw tantrum about how "Terraria did it first so no one else should be allowed to implement features this way". But when I take take every little bit that makes this game and look at them as a system, I can't see anything other than Terraria. The only difference is that it is top-down rather than sidescroll. Oh wait, no. There is another one. It is worse in every way. Yes, Terraria has 10+ more years since release compared to this game, which got a 1.0 version a month ago (at the moment of writing this review). Yet, considering how one can see the primary inspiration this game has in every pixel, why not borrow something more important? Fluid controls with shortcuts. Biome-specific loot tables for chests. More interesting enemies rather than hordes of bullet-spongy shamblers who do nothing but waste your health, ammo and, most importantly, your time (that's because they don't drop anything 4 times out of 5). I haven't even gotten to the first boss of the game, because the only weapon I looted in my 6.5 hours of gameplay is some stone-flinging hammer that had the same damage as a ninja star. What I got in droves is a Mysterious Portal. Which summons the first boss. Game really wanted to make sure I had a dozen Mysterious Portals at my base rather than weapons and trinkets to fight it. Then I found underground desert biome, which had noticeably harder enemies. I found a chest there, opened it and it had the exact same loot as a chest I found walking 10 meters from a spawn point. My game-closing thought was "What's the damn point?".  Mayhaps I am too harsh to someone's passion project that's been in development for quite a while now. So, here's truly personal nitpick to the game. Spritework and attack animation reminded me too much of Stardew Valley. And I DESPISE Stardew Valley.

If a game had all these features but had a less clunky combat and inventory experience it would have something.  i simply cannot stand the lousy clunky combat and the terraria-like inventory system.  == tried again in 2026 ==  As someone who does not like Terraria, I am really really not the audience for this game.

This game initially hooked me because it takes features from other popular games like Terraria, Rimworld, and Core Keeper. Unfortunately, the experience began to flatten after going through the initially enjoyable early stage. Overall, progression seemed pretty simple despite the actual rate of progression feeling drawn out due to combat and movement feeling pretty clunky. I never really struggled, but I never felt satisfied with my power level. Enemies and bosses were fairly consistently spongey.  By the time I reached the end, I knew I was uninterested in continuing with the endgame loop which is performing incursions repeatedly. These are basically instanced maps with variable gameplay modifiers. But outside of that, there was really nothing else to do. Despite having an infinite map, I didn't feel incentivized to explore beyond what I had already seen.  While this game has potential, I personally don't want to review games based on their potential (especially post early-access) and instead what is actually delivered. Based on what we have and what other similar games can offer, I would ultimately just recommend playing those instead to experience a more content-heavy, refined experience.

TLDR: Game is amazing but the late-game ruins the whole thing, and amplifies every little issue the game has. Play with friends or suffer an endless and tedious grind... or just quit after the mid-game.    As much as I loved the early-mid game, the end game tries to do something different and falls completely on its face. By the time I got to the final boss, I was begging for the game to be over with- I would've stopped playing if I hadn't already sucked so much time into it.   The incursion system is an interesting idea, one that I found interesting at first- but the more I had to grind the more I hated the game. I had to grind like 12 incursions to get a single crystal tablet. The sun arena boss is terrible, and his fists often bug out and teleport directly into me with no warning. Playing summoner became miserable as I had to switch to lower-tier weapons solely due to their better summon AI/targeting. The crystal resilience armor sounded cool, but the second I started using it, it flopped on its face and made my play style turn into a miserable slog.      I didn't care for any of the settler/building aspects of the game: it was easier to just travel to nearby villages and use their mages and traders for what I needed. Additionally, I would usually ask villagers to come to my settlement because why not, until a raid (which are complete trash by the way) killed all of them and their dead bodies are still lying on the ground, and I'm not bothered to slowly nudge them out of my house because there isn't way to get rid of them other than go on a tedious fetch quest to get revive potions. The enchanting mechanic is somehow more miserable than the one in Terraria- tinkering is way more expensive and way less rewarding. It squanders potential for different playstyles and reduces the amount of fun you'll have to pure luck. It took 5 incursions and 20k coins just to try and get a single enchantment I was looking for, just for me to have to replace it because theres way too many armor progressions that make previous tiers obsolete.    Lots of the incursion bosses are cool, but its ridiculous how bad they get when you have to fight them over and over. Spider Queen is in walls half of the time, and I don't have the resources time or money to keep blasting open temporary arenas to kill her faster. The crystal dragon boss is terribly telegraphed and mixes horrifically with the background of the incursion. I don't think there's been a single fight in any game where I enjoyed fighting a bosses minions, and this game stands as a testament to that.    Swamp Pest could've been an interesting fight but it just wasn't- i felt like the fight was a dice roll depending on where it came from and how big your arena is, mixed with how OP your weapons are at the point in time.    The issue with Swamp Pest was only amplified by the fact the boss summon item required an item from a creature from a sub-biome that was not only hard to find, but when you do find it, it requires literal hours of running back and forth, praying the creatures will spawn and drop enough Silk to allow you to craft a SINGLE attempt at a dice-roll boss with terrible AI and core mechanics.    At least with the Empress of Light (Terraria), the boss was tough but it was ALWAYS the players fault- every move in that boss fight was well-telegraphed and enjoyable, even when you lose to it. However, EOL also suffered from the issue of having to grind for a chance-related item in order to have another go at a difficult boss fight. I thought it was a terrible mechanic in Terraria, and I found it to be an even more terrible mechanic in Necesse.    Why can't you craft enchantments? Why can't you craft gateway tablets? Why can't you craft all the games trinkets instead of having to wander endlessly though the same biome hoping you'll see it in a random chest? Why can't you just let settlers die and remove their corpses from your house? Why is it necessary to make a pickaxe for every biome when you can just use dynamite to skip the tool progression entirely? Why is enchanting locked behind a settler? Why does food expire? Why shouldn't the player be able to attack through walls? Why do the summons have drastically different AI that completely changes the intended summoner progression? Why are the "raids" so terrible and contain nothing but NPC reskins with reused weapons? Why can't you fish the same biome-related fish from underground? Why don't spiders spawn as much as every other enemy in the game? Why is there a crazy influx of enemies when incursion objectives are almost completed? Why are incursion metals locked behind the "extraction" objectives only? Why does the health potion hotkey prioritize the lowest-tier potions? Why is the players hitbox so weird and hard to control? Why does Spider Queen have that near-impossible to dodge attack? Why is there not a potion to regain resilience? Why does it take so long to regenerate resilience when sleeping? Why does the Skeleton Staff suck so much? Why is the sun arena so bright that it makes it difficult to even see the boss, which is the exact same color as the floor? Why would it target summons onto NPCs instead of talking to them when you hold a summon weapon? Why does enchanting cost SO much? Why would the carpenter bench upgrade be so convoluted? Why can't omni-crystal convert into other essences? Why do you have to re-fight bosses if the Elder quest wasn't accepted beforehand? Why doesn't dynamite sync with your highest tool but bombs do? Why is there only like 2 treasure dungeon types, and why can you just run past and skip the both of them and tank the damage? Why do flamelings projectiles go through walls? Why do banners need a stand to be placed? Why do pawnbrokers have shop wealth- matter of fact, why do ANY traders have shop wealth? Why are none of the late game tools faster than the mid game ones? Why do the overworld biomes even really exist if they serve little to no purpose for the games progression? Why does the elder stop giving you progression quests when the incursions start? Why does farmland require so much wood?     I usually write stupid steam reviews for fun, but the endgame of this pissed me off so much. I was grinding the hell out of this game at first, and I loved every second of it, but the incursions ruined the entire experience and I think it would've been better if the game ended after the pre-incursion wizard fight, or just did a similar boss progression gauntlet like the rest of the game. It disguises a sell-out way to reuse boss fights as a terrible but unique end-game to an otherwise amazing game.

Heartbreaking, waited for months before I try out this game, thinking it's finally ready with 1.0 and would be a good time. It just.. isn't. It had a strong start, the first boss was simple but great, being a decently challenging but not unfair fight considering you have limited mobility at this point. Flawed yes with some telegraph (fireball rain) not really that well done. It went downhill from there. The game shows you the gameplay/progression loop pretty much immediately. Go to new biome, get the summon item which usually means mining around a bit for new ores/materials (skippable by sending miner to errands instead, because beating a boss unlocks next ore tier mining task), sometimes find the summon area, kill the boss, repeat the cycle. There's almost never any twist. It just ends up repetitive and boring. The biomes are essentially same ish as well. The bossfights are pretty much easy and not really that notable, a lot of the unique things just feels like gimmick instead of a mechanic. Also, I play melee and it's generally pretty bad in boss fights except for one weapon, disproportionately so compared to other classes because the bosses are generally so mobile you need dash to catch up, and projectiles originate from bosses so you cant dodge as well on top of running out of dash. The other thing is that how much the game copies stuff from terraria, often items/bosses/mechanics doing exactly the same things. It goes from "cool it's like in terraria" to "can they get more blatant? cant they be more original?" over time. The only part that feels unique and well done is the settlement system, but at the same time it feels like they're ultimately just resource generation/automation disguised. And there's less control over it unlike, say, palworld, where you can assign specific tasks.  For what it's worth, it's cheap, and there's still a decent game here. I just can't recommend it. The best part of the game is how it makes me crave more terraria for combat/exploration and minecraft for automation again.

I wanted to like this game because I like the idea of what it's going for, like terraria but top down and more able to automate the boring resource collecting, which sounds great on paper  But I just found it to be too much and too confusing. There's an overwhelming amount of different items, crafting benches, in game menus and settings. The settler system, while interesting, feels incredibly complicated. I also feel like the game doesn't explain itself super well. For example, I crafted a cooking pot which it told me to place on top of a campfire, I tried placing it by hand, right-clicking from my inventory, left-clicking, I could not figure out how it wanted me to place it and it seems like it just didn't work? So I eventually just gave up. Another example was that one of the settlers was hungry and it told me to mark a chest with food in it as a chest so that they can access it to get food. I did exactly what it told me clicking one of the chest buttons, and it just... didn't work? Like it looked how it should in the tutorial, but then my settler just didn't ever go to the chest? I spent 5 minutes trawling through the various menus associated with the settlement and the chest and couldn't figure it out, so again I just gave up  Also I just gotta say that the hunger system is terrible. Already I think that hunger bars in games are, at best, tedious, and at worst, frustrating. I can't imagine playing terraria and saying "man if only there was a hunger bar I had to manage to constantly interrupt me from all the fun exploring, then this game would be a 10/10!". In this game it's the same except it's way worse because, firstly you have to manage not only your hunger but the hunger of all of the settlers too, and secondly the food expires, so once you have it in your inventory there's a real time limit to eat it otherwise it goes off. Now this seems to be pretty generous, like 5 real life hours, so it's not super pressing, but it's very annoying that the only mechanism to get rid of the tedium that is managing a hunger bar, which is stacking up loads of food, seems to not be viable in this game?  To try to be fair, I remember I felt similar when I played terraria for the first time, that I felt overwhelmed by how many items and events and crafting stations and npcs that there were, but everyone kept saying how good it is so I pushed through and I'm glad that I did. I imagine that Necesse could be the same, and if it becomes really big and everyone starts saying how good it is then I might give it another go and push through. However, as of right now I just think it's too complicated and long-winded for me to bother with. I can definitely see why it would appeal to certain people though

The devs played Terraria, Minecraft, Rimworld... And they fell in love with the surface, apparently, while missing the actual depth.  For example: there's wires. They're clearly copied from terraria. They come in 4 colors. You can connect them to logic gates, even to counters, and timers, and delays... But what I/O is there? - Motion sensor - Music block - Led panel  That's IT! That's all you can do with all that Turing-complete logic. Enjoy making Darude Sandstorm or a 4-bit adder for no ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ reason I guess, since you can't use all that logic to actually affect the world besides triggering traps.  Base building is so secondary that I got an achievement for killing an enemy with a trap, and only 0.6% of all players got it, demonstrating that it's all really just an afterthought.  The whole game is an afterthought.

Purchased in early 2022 while still in very Early Access. Total playtime nearly 150 hours (including offline playtime).  [b]TL;DR I do mostly recommend the game up until Incursions with the understanding you may want to stop then, but this locks you out of about a third of the game unless you want to mindlessly grind for bigger numbers and stop interacting with the rest of the game.[/b]  [h1]Pros:[/h1]  + Slightly simplified top-down Terraria + simplified Rimworld. The mix of colony management optimization and Terraria-style progression is addicting in every way. If you enjoy anything close to Terraria, this is an instant buy.  + The soundtrack has its own vibe that's hard to describe. It's good... perhaps fewer epic highs compared to other games, but the overall floor is higher. The first boss' song is an absolute banger... and it's just the first boss.  + The new seamless world gen is so much nicer than the island style of Early Access. Just a straight upgrade.  [h1]Cons:[/h1]  - The first and most serious con, and the ultimate tipping point for a negative review, is the Incursion system. For most of the game, you're playing Terraria+Rimworld. Once you complete the "main story" missions, the game turns into an oversimplified Diablo/Path of Exile game. You run tablets (maps/rifts) that have random modifiers to get a few types of currencies. You use these currencies to linearly increase the stats of your existing gear. At the end of the tablets, you fight a boss which is usually poorly balanced and either super under or overpowered relative to your current power level. Rinse and repeat, grind, grind, grind. You can basically ignore your settlement and the rest of the game at this point.  - Difficulty settings feel under-cooked and tacked on. There are no mechanical differences with combat or boss mechanics between the difficulties, only "things hit harder" and "you can't revive settlers if they die."   - At v1.0.2, there are still quite substantial balancing issues. Oftentimes I would upgrade to a newer weapon that did less damage than the previous one even taking into account AOE/piercing abilities. Some weapons in mid game I ended up keeping until the very end.  - Significant performance issues later on. Running a 13600k and 9070 XT at 1440p and I can't get more than 120 fps (usually bouncing from 80-120) at end game with a single settlement. During the penultimate boss fight, I even dropped to consistently below 60 fps. Max single CPU core usage around 20% which points to optimization issues. This game shouldn't have a problem running at a locked high refresh rate on a weaker system, let alone mine.  - Most of the bosses are... bland and simple, I guess? In Early Access this was forgivable because I expected changes to be made, but every boss boils down to three things: classic worm boss (easy to defeat with piercing weapons to ignore all mechanics), a magic boss that circles around you and shoots projectiles (just circle them back and attack with a homing weapon until you win), or a boss that charges/rams you in a straight line (run in a circle to avoid and attack with a homing weapon until you win). One of the later bosses changes it up slightly by making you play tic-tac-toe against them while also dodging projectiles which was well designed, but it's the only boss with anything interesting going on.  - In addition to the previous point, bosses are also bland in the sense that the fights aren't much of a spectacle, even the final "main story" boss. While this game isn't Terraria, it's probably the best comparison so I'll use it. The first time you fight bosses like the Wall of Flesh, the Empress of Light, Celestial Pillars/Moon Lord, and others feels like an epic war of attrition. Here, there was very little difference between the third wizard and final wizard bosses other than the arena location.

I’ve had a good amount of fun with Necesse, especially in the early stages. The settlement-building system is one of the game’s strongest points — it’s satisfying to recruit NPCs, set up houses, and assign them to different jobs. Having workers take care of farming, crafting, and other tasks gives the world a nice sense of life and progression.  That said, once you get past the early game, the progression starts to feel less rewarding. After a while, it feels like you’re just moving from boss to boss without much need for mining, crafting, or meaningful exploration in between. The gear upgrades and rewards start to feel less impactful, and the mid-to-late game loop doesn’t quite capture the same sense of discovery as the beginning.  For the sale price of around $10, Necesse is definitely worth picking up — there’s plenty of charm and solid mechanics to enjoy. But with just the 1.0 content, it left me wanting more. The foundation is great, but it feels like there’s a missing layer of depth or incentive to keep pushing forward once you’ve seen most of what the game offers.

Gameplay loop becomes super bland very quickly as it is designed to force you into making settlements, which becomes a problem in Multiplayer as it removes quite a lot that a player can do. In Singleplayer I guess its okay, but villager micromanaging hell is not for me.  The game very heavily leans towards a summoner playstyle, with there being a lot of trinkets and armor sets that buff / increase summons as well as being able to recruit party member NPC's. (which you will also have to micromanage)  In general, there is no real sense of progression, accomplishment or even acknowledgement from the game's world- you simply go into a new biome every time, acquire biome-specific minerals, kill a boss specific to the biome and repeat until you reach the end of the game. I didn't feel satisfied when I killed the first boss for the first time.  There is more engagement and recognition of your actions in Terraria than in this game, as in that game you will literally cause raids, events, new ores and new monsters to appear with (almost) each boss fight you've fought off.  None of this happens in this game, which is funny considering that this is the [i]only[/i] thing they were unable to replicate after taking so many ideas from Terraria.  I don't think Necesse even knows what it wants to be at this point.

The game is honestly amazing, until you reach incursions. Up to that point, it somewhat captures Terraria's sense of exploration and progression, with clear goals, and a similar feeling of discovery. But once incursions begin, all of that falls apart.  At that stage, the game turns into a grind for what’s essentially currency, used to push your gear into higher tiers (Which does not feel rewarding). You’re stuck replaying the same map layouts over and over, doing boring objectives like mining ore you no longer need or killing a certain number of enemies.  And the "Infinite Seamless World" quickly loses its meaning once you’ve cleared each biome, there’s simply no reason to go back. This problem becomes even worse once you reach incursions, as you’re no longer in the open world at all, you’re just interacting with an altar you place in your base that removes the open world entirely. Which leaves the endgame feeling like a boring and dumbed down version of PoE, with none of the depth that makes PoE engaging.
