A game that is too much like its inspiration, Terraria, without really improving upon the gameplay loop in any way. I would argue that most of it is just downright worse but I wouldn't call this a bad game by any means, I did enjoy my time with my friends, but that's not enough for me to warrant a suggestion that others play this game.   Like terraria the combat loop in this game is mine stuff, build stuff, fight enemies/bosses, repeat. Unlike Terraria the sense of progression in this game is very shallow and there is no real movement going on in the world. The game refuses to change up its rules or alter the gameplay AT ALL in order to shake things up. For the period of literally the entirety of the game it goes as follows:  1. Mine in new area to get ore unique to that biome  2. Build new pickaxe with the aformentioned ore to break be able to break the next biomes blocks  3. Repeat  There are of course bosses unique to each biome and they're alright, kind of easy if I will be honest but I did enjoy the pursuit of trying to kill them all. That desire quickly fades when you realize that the cycle I mentioned before is entirely unchanging and it just becomes a slog. Each new biome is basically a reskin of the last with very minimal differences. There are unique enemies but they're mostly, (but not entirely), the same thing even lacking lots of visual flair to even make them distinctive. The actual tile-sets themselves I think are lackluster but I may be conflating this thought with just how samey the gameplay is between the biomes.  Terraria is not a game I love to death, but it is a game I respect. Each biome/region has such distinctive visual and gameplay differences from each other that it makes you have to change your approach upon entering them. Once you beat Wall of Flesh suddenly hard mode occurs and you are showered with such crazy ♥♥♥♥ constantly, and the rules change (like corruption spreading) where it switches up your goals. That game forces you to adapt and fight for your goals. Necesse has  at most a minimal amount of that. I was hoping when I unlocked the deep cave ladder that it was basically the "hard mode transition" that Terraria has but nope, it's the same ♥♥♥♥ but now there is lava in some places. Boring, not entertaining, barely an obstacle.  I think why there isnt a sense of world progression is because Terraria has this problem where people who join a world late in have this feeling of missing out on the events that previously occurred, especially hard mode and on. It does have a linear path to advance the world and once it's been tread that's it. I think the Necesse devs recognized that issue and made Necesse about individual progression rather than a world progression. An interesting idea but they didn't solve more issues than they made, and as a result they made a mediocre experience. This is made more obvious where after mining and upgrading your stuff when you leave the mine everything on the surface is completely trivial in terms of difficulty. That's a shame.  The settlement system is fine but there's really not much to it, it sort of just exists but it's not bad. I'm sure as time goes on they will flesh out that system but I don't have many complaints beyond "needs greater depth and focus."  The art and music is fine but I don't like any of it. Personal preference thing, not a real criticism. I think some of the sprite work is a little too cluttered and big.   I don't like the UI at all even if I admit it's better than the Terraria UI in many ways. I think it lacks a lot of personality and is just really cluttered but still readable. The UI is especially messy to navigate with a controller and I'd like to see them improve the controller support but this is a minor complaint.   Necesse is a fine game but I already own Terraria, there is no reason to come back to this game.

I did want to give this game as much of a chance as I could, so I've reached the endgame, done a few tablets (their version of maps from path of exile) and I am quite bored unfortunately. The game suffers from some design decisions I'm not a big fan of personally.  So the game is a mix between core keeper, terraria and some farming/city builder sim mixed in one. It definitely takes a LOT of inspiration from terraria, with many items doing the exact same thing as their terraria counterpart. This is great, I like terraria. However, it fails to capture what truly makes terraria fun and replayable.  Firstly, character movement throughout the world. In the endgame of terraria, you will ride a UFO blasting through the world with infinite explosions - nothing gets in your way. In this game, you can get a good amount of movement speed but... just walk in any direction and you bump into a tree. Or a rock. You can't RUN through the overworld without constantly bumping into things. The game NEEDS to make trees intangible so you can actually move in the overworld uninterrupted.  Speaking of the world, I'm not sure why they hyped up infinite worlds so much. I have currently explored all the biomes in a smallish area around my spawn, faught all the cave bosses, got most of the items. I literally have no reason to ever explore the world infinitely as the content now is just the endgame maps which Ive set up at my homebase. This to me feels like a lack of understanding as to what actually makes games fun - take terraria for example, the game they are inspired by. Terraria doesn't have an infinite world, because it would be pointless after a certain point, and the world it has is filled with everything you need to beat the game. And if you are missing some optional items like a desert pyramid, you would just generate a new world! On top of this, when you reach hardmode in terraria you would constantly revisit old areas to fight the new mobs and mine the new ore, truly utilising the finite world you created. In this game, it's pretty much one and done (maybe they'll make a hardmode in the future?) because you visit a biome once, kill the boss, and theres no reason to really go there again if you got all the drops. In my opinion it should have just been a single, large world with all the biomes and that would have been enough.  Regarding the endgame, I am so BORED. I've done like 4 maps in a row all forcing me to mine 100 or so blocks of ancient ore. WHY. I don't NEED anymore ancient ore, I already have thousands! There's nothing I can do with this ore except sell it! On top of this, there's no super FAST way to blast through these levels, even with bombs, dynamite and a tier 10 pickaxe with a mining trinkets it still takes too long. I was stuck in a map for an uncomfortable amount of time because I missed an ore vein. (if anyone played path of exile 2, do you remember when players complained about this sort of thing, where they almost complete a map but miss one enemy at the beginning of the map and have to backtrack the whole map trying to find the enemy? This is kinda similar). It would be much better if the game was able to select which kind of incursion we want to generate, like a straight up boss arena where you just fight the boss and get rewards, because mining ore to complete the map is getting reaaaal old. The boss fights themselves are kinda fun, even if they are just the same bosses again and again with "number go up".  Even with all the weapons and items, the combat in the game is less complex that you would think. The game has hordes of enemies swarm you. There are weapons that can only attack one single enemy at a time, and weapons with piercing. You pretty much have to use the piercing weapons/spells otherwise you it would take forever to fight all the enemies. Theres even a boss thats a swarm of bats with each bat having its own health bar. GOOD LUCK trying to use a single target weapon. I understand "different sets/tools for different jobs" but in 90% of cases a piercing weapon is the best choice. And also, summons are ok but the ones that can't phase through walls are pretty useless as they have to walk around blocks which means even with a high summon speed they can take forever to pathfind.  So yeah, that's about it. There's some minor stuff as well like how I hardly interacted with the villagers after a certain point, and the music is kinda annoying after a while, and the lighting is not that good. It's way too dark half the time.  My final verdict would be... try it out as a lot of people seem to like it and its quite cheap on sale. But if you played terraria for hundreds of hours, you may feel frustrated with certain aspects and systems here. I think with time it could become great with updates, so maybe I'll check back in the future.

I'm a huge fan of Terraria. So when I bought this I was hoping for a similar experience... sadly, it's not so.  The controls are janky. QoL stuff that Terraria spoiled me doesn't exist here (you need a DEDICATED key for placing torches? FO).  The boss fights are also boring, I don't know but in Terraria they always posed some challenge, you'd maybe prepare an arena for them. Or switch between worlds to gear up and take on the boss, here... You just spam some summon weapon because it's basically homing missiles against the boss and try to avoid things... Oh, and attacking while moving makes you move slower... bleh.  Then there's the colony mechanic, but it lacks any depth by the looks of it... Like, you have some reason to craft more stuff to gear your colonists, but I don't see a huge reason for it.  Overall, it's a grindy game with little depth and very little/no reward for exploration. Gave Necesse the ole college try, and it didn't grow on me. I hopped into the game and had a dictionary thrown at me from the get go with a trillion different things to account for - crafting materials, work stations, NPCs, etc - instead of starting the game simple and increasing complexity as time goes on. Too much too quick. The elder (an actual living encyclopedia), had a ton of stuff to explain, but that's "telling" rather than "showing". It's like one fat unappealing lore log that the player always skips in a game. Videogames are an interactive medium, and having to read a small novel instead of having it play out with player participation is not a good tutorial.  The fun (finally) started in the underground, where I fought enemies and gathered gold, ore, trinkets, and other items... but it was like gorging myself on candy for a short sugar rush, and eventually after gathering 4,000 gold in what feels like the very early game, it occurred to me that maybe there are more impactful things to do in the game, as gold felt too easy to obtain. Honestly I think finding 3 piles of 20 gold each would be much better than 20 piles of 3 gold each.  Finally, after some exploring of the main map, I found the pirate town and got gibbed multiple times, and the two consecutive trips back to pick up my stuff made me realize the game just isn't for me. Necesse already feels too much like a top-down Terraria, and unfortunately that's what I feel compelled to play while playing Necesse.  To sum it up, the beginning of the game overwhelms you, then entertains you for a half hour, then bores you to tears from then on. Not for me.

I’ve had a good amount of fun with Necesse, especially in the early stages. The settlement-building system is one of the game’s strongest points — it’s satisfying to recruit NPCs, set up houses, and assign them to different jobs. Having workers take care of farming, crafting, and other tasks gives the world a nice sense of life and progression.  That said, once you get past the early game, the progression starts to feel less rewarding. After a while, it feels like you’re just moving from boss to boss without much need for mining, crafting, or meaningful exploration in between. The gear upgrades and rewards start to feel less impactful, and the mid-to-late game loop doesn’t quite capture the same sense of discovery as the beginning.  For the sale price of around $10, Necesse is definitely worth picking up — there’s plenty of charm and solid mechanics to enjoy. But with just the 1.0 content, it left me wanting more. The foundation is great, but it feels like there’s a missing layer of depth or incentive to keep pushing forward once you’ve seen most of what the game offers.

Necesse is a promising game, but it has been in early access for 6 years at this point. What it has to offer is lackluster:   The settlement system is the main selling point I guess, but it's really barebones. It's not like in Rimworld or Oxygen Not Included, where your colonists/duplicants have to perform everything and your job is to utilize and optimize their labour. The item storage system is based on chests that are slightly bigger than the ones in Terraria, you have the option to craft from nearby containers, quick stack to nearby containers, and your colonists can sort your chests after you tediously set up filters and priorities. You can give a workbench work orders and a colonist will come to perform them; the interface is the same as Rimworld, but the implementation is closer to Dwarf Fortress. Colonists can also butcher animals (kinda the same as DFHack's autobutcher feature), harvest crops and replant crops (including trees) (exactly the same as Dwarf Fortress), and fish (same as Dwarf Fortress).  The combat is really bad. You get slowed for using your weapon, which feels clunky. The devs saw how Terraria's Master Mode Wall of Flesh gets faster the lower hp it has and implemented this to almost every single boss. This results in bosses feeling sluggish in the first phase, and get impossible to dodge under 5% hp. Boss HP is overly inflated to the point where every bossfight lasts 10-15 minutes, and you have to constantly dodge, because every bossfight is a bullet hell, this gets worse if you dare to use your weapon, which slows you down. The weapons feel really bad to use, and most of them are ripped straight from Terraria or a Terraria mod. The potion system is the exact same found in Terraria, they even have the spelunker potion. The "enchant" feature is the reforge feature ripped from Terraria, instead of the goblin, you have the mage who will enchant your equipment... for coins, and this is the biggest money sink in the game, the same as in Terraria. Accessories are really basic and they don't drastically change your playstyle, and bosses drop consumables that increase your accessory count... the same as WoF's Demon Heart drop in Terraria. Each area has its own ore, and you can make tools and armor out of them; some armors are multi-class, and you change which class it affects by... changing the helmet, the same way you would do in Terraria. You see the pattern.  Progression is kinda based on areas with their own bosses, but nothing is really locked like in Valheim (where the first boss gates the ability to mine, and you need the boss' drops for the next area). The quests are just a boss checklist, but they guide you through the intended biome progression. The first progression gate is that the pirate captain drops the first deep ladder, which unlocks deep cave biomes, and the second gate is that the fallen wizard drops the first gateway tablet, which unlocks incursions. All the other bosses are optional.  Incursions are unique in the sense that are ripped from MMOs instead of survival games. They are the endgame content, you get your first gateway tablet from the fallen wizard, and after that you get more from within the incursions themselves. They are broken into tiers, like in Path of Exile. Each gateway tablet can have it's own unique affix, like in Path of Exile. You can upgrade a gateway tablet into the next tier, like in Path of Exile. Path of Exile of course has a more complex map system, and Necesse's incursion system is sluggish and the objectives are extremely basic, like "kill 100 enemies", or "mine 100 ores", or "kill the boss", or... that's it, there are only 3 objective types. They add 6 unique biomes, which is nice. The loot system in them is pretty bad, for example, to make an incursion weapon, like the Slime Bow, you need to roll for the Slime Cave biome, and then roll for to have the "kill 100 enemies" objective, complete it, then roll again for the Slime Cave biome, and then now for it to have the "mine 100 ores" objective. Only after you complete a biome twice with both "kill" and "mine", can you craft the respective equipment.   The building system is ripped from Prison Architect/Rimworld with a lot less complexity and buildings.  The music is nice, it never felt looping and samey. The sound effects could be better, and the audio balancing can be really off. The textures are alright after the graphics update, the animations are really bad, and the lighting system looks like if you disabled smooth lighting in Minecraft.  They used AI for translations, which makes them useless.  There are also some bugs, like FPS dropping when you open the chat for some reason.  The entire game feels like it was trying to be a Terraria mod with some barebones Dwarf Fortress elements that are reskinned to look like they were ripped from Rimworld. Anything truly unique the game does is bad. I would recommend playing the games that this game is based on: Terraria, Dwarf Fortress, Rimworld. Core Keeper is also an honorable mention.

For a game that really had me thinking "Hey it's just Terraria with a underwhelming paint job." I was shocked by the amount of detail, countless updates, and content this game has to offer. It's certainly not as "basic" as I assumed it would be, but despite all this I just still can't get around how similar this game feels compared to it's clear inspiration and ideas.   Unassuming comes to mind when first looking at Necesse. I have no bloody clue why it's named that, what I'm doing here, or why I'm doing anything, but I certainly have a lot I CAN do so that's a good place to start as any. It hankers back to various titles akin to it's very similar gameplay and art style. Unmistakably so even.  I built a shack, dealt with the undead night, spoke to NPC's who wanted to take my shack, and fought monsters inside of a dungeon eventually culminating to me fighting a huge field boss type monster that took me down in a matter of seconds. Knowing this I built my shack to become a decent livable home, gave those NPC"s their own dang rooms rather than my own, built armor and weapons that could take down more than your common skeleton, and had a small farm raising chickens to get food for my newly upcoming reunion with the field boss once again, whom I this time promptly defeated and he spits out an NPC.   Get my point yet?  Nearly all of that sounds familiar from one title or another, and all extremely popular ones at that. That's the core problem, it feels all too similar to games that clearly inspired it, and even if they had not the game feels too similar to ignore these comparisons. And while I will never say that's a bad thing, it just feels this game is lacking in comparison to those.   I hardly feel compelled to really do anything, and the game never really defines what it really wants to be. Terraria for example is considered the 2D Minecraft of the Steam world, yet it stands on it's own without question. I would choose Terraria over Minecraft anyday, and simple comparisons to the two hardly do either of them justice.   And while clearly the graphics update helped Necesse start to stand on it's own at least visually in a sense, the gameplay is still lacking to compel me to enjoy it over others I would easily go back to time and time again as beloved classics.   That's honestly not an easy thing to create, and I hope one day Necesse achieves this triumphed glory if the developers are truly aiming for the same heights. Yet as it stands It just isn't that kind of game for me. Maybe for others, but I for one just wish for something more distinct, and something that I can say only Necesse can do.

Necesse does it all, but does not perform at any thing exceptionally well. It is a very surface- level game, which lacks any new or interesting game mechanic, enemy design, progression or world building, to be engaging.   Necesse has:  Don't Starve building Terraria-like trinkets, weapons, armour Stardew Valley farming Rimworld management  The biomes this game has are lacking. Most biome ores are locked behind a better pickaxe. Imagine Terraria, but you could go anywhere on the map immediately, with the starting pickaxe. That's Necesse, with it's abruptly spread-out biome progression. You go from biome to biome, farm resources (and sometimes you don't, because you can't mine there!), and once you have enough to craft a better pick, or an armor, you have no need to return to those "weaker" biomes, since the ores and materials they offer fall short the further you are into the game.  The Difficulty setting is purely adjusting the health and damage of the enemies, nothing gets added, you become weaker in return.  The enemies in the game world are mostly 2 generic types - the "melee guy", tries ramming into you, and the "shoot" guy, shoots you while going into a straight line or standing still. Worm enemies go through terrain, which is not bad, and is well executed.   Bosses are VERY repetitive, only having 2-4 attack patterns, they are simply sped up when get low on health. Some of those attack patterns are nearly impossible to dodge when sped up, no matter how well you know them.  Someday you get raided by [strike]illagers[/strike] raiders, which target you and your settlement's locals. The raids offer no real challenge, but are very annoying, because you need to wait for the enemies to arrive, and then slaughter them. They offer no real reward by themselves, but sometimes drop a really powerful equipment. Raids can be turned off, so there's good in that.  This review was updated for 1.1. My statements stay unchanged.

Played during free weekend.    The game isn't bad, but honestly it feels very, very... confused. On one hand you've got an RPG game like terraria where you adventure, kill enemies, explore and progress to different areas, weapons, materials, etc. On the other you have a town building game. But the two... are not connected. The villagers i found (playing with a friend) never really offered anything notable. Cool, they can do a bit of woodcutting, or a bit of mining, or get a bit of food (which the villagers all eat). It all feels like a side diversion that's not actually related, nor helpful in any way. Honestly, I don't really get how this game has such a high number of positive reviews. These are my biggest complaints so far: - The progression system comes straight out of Terraria, but is also incredibly and unnecessarily sped up. I quite easily killed 3 bosses in less than 6 hours. This goes hand-in-hand with acquiring and combining trinkets way too quickly. The feeling of "♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, this is a huge step and I really had to work for it" is therefore completely abscent. - The map system is simply not interesting. You go from (randomly generated) island to island, but what does it really add to the experience in comparison to the classic "one map" principle? Except for quicker traversal (you can fast travel to previously discovered places), not a lot and I'm not even sure if this is an upgrade from the classic formula. - The combat is nothing we haven't seen before and has clearly been executed more successfully elsewhere. The AI is nothing to write home about and most ennemies just run straight up to you without much of an unexpected pattern.  TL:DR - I feel like this game was heavily inspired by Terraria without coming close to it's excellent and perfectly balanced execution. Although it adds some new ideas (the town folk management system is OK), it doesn't elevate it to the level of previous entries in the genre. I'd rather go play another round of Terraria or Core Keeper.

This game feels like it doesn't take itself seriously. The hardest difficulty is trivially easy for most of the game. It becomes challenging near the end when enemies kill you in 2-3 hits, but even then it doesn't feel difficult considering you just pick up your gear and try again, or just wait for your friend to respawn to rejoin the fight. I also read you can bring NPC's into the fights with you to make them easier, but I never attempted this.  The progression system feels like the biggest missed opportunity. It feels like the developers put together a satisfying progression system, felt it was too grindy, and dumbed it down into a the most unsatisfyingly trivial system imaginable. Everything is handed to you on a silver platter. Nothing feels like a "quest" to obtain. It is jarring and sudden how quickly you progress through the game tiers. It does not feel good spending 20+ hours on a game that could likely be beaten in 3-4 hours if you don't bother with the city building side of things just because the developers were afraid to put in any grind or struggle.   You have an extremely large world to explore, but you will be done exploring after four islands. There is no benefit to making an outpost on these islands or interact with them beyond the bare minimum you need to find trinkets and unlock the boss. The "Exploration" aspect of this game may as well be non-existent.  Knowing what I know now, I never would have purchased this.

Overall this game is very very fun.   It has tons of depth and management of settlers is fun (coming from rim world experience)   Where it starts dragging is in its late game progression. Everything progression wise is fun and fair until you unlock incursions. You have hundreds of tools at your disposal which makes managing the pre end game bosses exciting and challenging. End game you either need to grind out several hours worth of ore and pots to upgrade and this is where the negative review comes from. Its such a step up in difficulty that anything outside of ranged and homing feels unabalanced and hard to manage.   Getting stuck on objects during these long and tedious fights is the worst feeling. You end up having to mine out giant sections of the map just to survive. Bosses however dont have to deal or interact with the same things we do. In that same regard it wouldnt be an issue if there was more ways to power up outside of boosting all your gear to tier 5 or spamming enchants.   Theres not any exploration in these end game zones. Lots of weapons and relics feel gimmicky despite supposedly being more powerful the prime example being the graveyard incursion blood weapons. Tanking doesnt feel fleshed out, magic feels horrible, summons are ok and ranged is easily the best subclass there is.   The only way I and my friends have progressed outside of grinding has been respawn cheesing.     I want to beat this game, I want to suggest you buy it but until things become more balanced in that final stretch i cannot recommend.

One thing kept going into my mind as I reached 2 hours in: [b]Where is the rest of this game?[/b]    Reviews calling it a mix of Terraria, Rimworld and Minecraft are doing the game a huge disservice because it simply just does not have even close to the amount of content. This game is overwhelmingly shallow, I would compare it to Forager far more than I would compare it to the other three.     You want to see the next large mountain to overcome, yet you're still running into the same enemies (sometimes recolored) and the gameplay loop hasn't changed, not in the way it would with Terraria where you're digging deeper, or with Minecraft where you're building higher, or with Rimworld where you've suddenly just had some random event drop on your lap and one of your pawns has absolutely had it because he's wearing human skin. This game lets itself be pulled in all directions that it's lost a sense of self. It is a smoothie that has so many fruits in it's this gray semi-fluid that tastes of only sugar. This game is having an identity crisis and it's so sad to see because it has potential to be good.    I'm not saying it needs more weapons, more animals, more pets, more enemies. What it needs is to be taken to a therapy session, sat down, and ask itself "Who am I? What do I want to be? Where do I go from here?" Once Necesse finds itself, truly, it could be an incredible game, but right now it is a gray smoothie.

Some people mention this game is like Terraria, Rimworld and Stardew Valley put together, but worse in every aspect. So..  That's partially true. But more precisely it's 80% Terraria and 20% Rimworld. I don't know about similarities from Stardew, but seems like it's just... tiles? Wait, but Rimworld also has some. Anyway, I managed to play through the first half (or third?) part of the game, beat the pirate captain and slowly found out this game completely lacks any unique features, it's literally Terraria clone, starting from progression system, trinkets, potion crafting, exploration in search for underground chests, up to certain UI design choices.   But it's not like Terraria successor, it's just simply not fun exploring the same underground biomes with reskins of 3-4 enemies, lacking any AI-behaviour depth, where one is running towards you like a magnet, another one just shoots a projectile and the third one is a slime, teleporting to you all of a sudden without proper animation.   Settlement control isn't interesting at all too because of the same issue of lacking depth, you really can just automate basic resource collecting, storage sorting and item crafting. No settler character traits, no need to manage them somehow, they are just NPCs from Terraria with different items for trade.  Overall I would heavily encourage everyone to play Terraria or any other sandbox game from thousands instead of this. You just won't find anything new and fresh enough to surprise you or grab your attention here.

I don't think I can recommend the game at full price. I bought the game when it was super sale, I think for like $4.99. That's a good price, I guess. After 5 years of EA there just does not seem to be any content. I am baffled by the reviews.     The first few hours of the game were an absolute blast. Figuring out how to hire new villagers, assigning them tasks, getting boxes autofilled... but then you realize that is it. I really, really tried to enjoy this game but it might be the easiest most content-less game ever made. I was playing this on the hardest settings. I died twice. in 61 hours. I don't know why I played this long. I literally just watched my village grow and produce things that offer no benefit. There is no reason to do any of it.    I am not sure why the reviews are so good. In this genre of games it is literally the easiest game with the most extreme lack of content. Seriously, after 3-4 hours you have pretty much seen it all. A real snoozer.    - Story: There is no story.    - Open world: I guess. So basically there are just a few "biomes" you can explore. And by "biome" I mean you can navigate to tiny little tiles on the map. Every biome is exactly the same. There is nothing special about the biomes except a new type of seed and then a biome specific cave. You will see all the biomes in the first 5 minutes of exploration.    - Combat: Kind of fun. Unfortunately this is not an RPG and no customization and no classes. Its fun for the first 15 minutes or so but every enemy is the same regardless of biome. A zombie in one spot, a zombie with bandages called a "mummy" in another spot. Weapons and armor have no variety.     - Settlement: There are 0 mechanics to the settlement. Not sure why it is even a part of the game. You just need to make sure the villagers have beds and furniture and that is it. They never die, they never need anything. Probably one of the worst settlement features ever implemented in the history of settlement features being in games.    - Loot: There is almost no loot to be found. Each biome that does not have a dungeon or village will have 1-2 storage chests or barrels. They have the same stuff. There are "enchantment" scrolls, but there the variety is so low one wonders what the dev could possibly be thinking. There is not much loot to be found. Bosses drop the same stuff every time you kill them. Zombies drop the same stuff. Unbelievable how little loot there is.    - Summary: If you want a game that has produced almost 0 content in 5 years, go for it. The dev must have been a rancher in their former lifetime as they sure know how to milk this one good.

I really need a "mixed" option for feedback. This isn't a bad game by any means, but compared to its genre? I think it's more that it doesn't excel at anything. It's adequate. It's competent. I can't really say anything positive that isn't damning it with faint praise. (Hence why I had to mark it as not recommended. Its biggest asset is the potential to grow into a standout entry, much like how Terraria went from "pixel Minecraft with bosses" into a deep and complex game whose layers unfold throughout the game and breathe new life into even already-explored parts of the map.)  I think the exploration almost has something to it, but having every section separated into these islands is something that made me not want to explore too much, honestly. I do not enjoy sailing or swimming in many of these Minecraft-esque survival crafting games, and Necesse is certainly no exception. I don't think the water should be removed by any means, as it certainly adds some visual flavor to the world and breaks up the wandering feeling so much like Don't Starve (which is a game I do not enjoy as much as my friends). Bear in mind that I only got four hours in, but I think there's room for more interesting travel mechanics. I know there are mine carts, and I find those to be the best way to get around of what I've seen. The devs won't want to copy Terraria's homework, but there are literally dozens of travel-related items in that game, some based on jumping, others on flying, some on running, some on mounts, some on simply tunneling through obstacles... Again, different game, and blatant copying would be stale, but I would encourage the devs to put some thought into localized travel. I know there's some long-distance QoL stuff already in the game, but I find local travel pretty dull, and I didn't feel particularly incentivized to travel too far beyond my base, the farm I set up, and the close-ish area underneath my base.  My other biggest gripe maybe just proves that this isn't a game made for me. I ended up accidentally dismissing the starting quest, which made it a lot slower for me to get my feet under me, but I did eventually do the starting quest objectives, just with a base that I made myself a few screens over. My first instinct was to exit the starting quest and just get to exploring, but... the game is kind of structured around quests from what I can tell, at least for a clearer path toward progression. That runs counter to how I'm used to playing these games, and I didn't enjoy it at all. Yet without quests and without enjoying exploring, I found it much harder to find a direction than I usually do. Usually, it boiled down to stumbling upon something in the crafting menu, thinking it looked cool, and figuring out how I get the materials. I got a spider summon! I love summoning minions! But it was a pretty short-lived high, and that was about where I tapped out.  Crafting has to get a mixed grade. It's good that there is so much information on what you can craft without having to search through wikis, but the way it was presented had me feeling overwhelmed with options. I'm sure this will be polished and improved over time, and maybe it already has in the two or three months since I've picked up this game.  I think the NPC recruiting might be where this game eventually shines. I liked that you can build up sort of a village and assign them jobs so they're specifically useful to your goals. To me, base-building was the most satisfying part of this game, and I could see myself becoming interested in expanding out forever from a central hub (assuming maps ever stop being all islands, of course).

After 14 hours I'm just kinda bored of the game, exploration feels pretty aimless & the town building stuff is pretty cool but ultimately not enough to keep me engaged and I find myself opting to do anything BUT hop on & play the game for the sake of "just explore until you find something interesting".    Will prolly check back sometime later in development but as of right now it's still TOO "Early Access" for me to get much enjoyment out of it.

I stumbled upon this game and decided to check out the reviews. They were overwhelmingly positive, with many claiming it's a cool blend of Terraria and RimWorld, featuring great music and more. So, I decided to purchase it. For the first five hours or so, the game held my interest. It did indeed have NPCs and mechanics reminiscent of RimWorld, and everything seemed mysterious and intriguing.  However, around the 10-15 hour mark, the once-cool music started to grate on me because it was the same tracks on repeat. Whether it's daytime or nighttime in your forest village, you're stuck listening to the same two melodies. Still, I tried to bear with it.  As you progress and expand your village, engaging in various management tasks like organizing chests and zones, you realize it's all smoke and mirrors. There's no RimWorld-esque depth here. Essentially, the NPCs' sole purpose is to help you sort chests, craft items, shear sheep, and milk cows. That's it. There's nothing else—no relationships between NPCs, no weddings, no special characteristics. All NPCs are just mindless minions performing primitive tasks. Sure, they have moods and might become discontent, but all they can do with their dissatisfaction is put up signs for the player to notice and then simply stop working. That's a real threat to the player, isn't it? I understand that merging Terraria with RimWorld's complex NPC system might be challenging since they are fundamentally different games, but why bother adding this content in the first place if it's just to save players the trouble of manually sorting chests? They could have come up with something more interesting. Right now, it just looks like a cheap knockoff of a well-known game.  Now, onto the gameplay itself. It's interesting, sure. There are bosses, some minor content, a class system of sorts, mages, archers, different biomes, and so on. They've clearly taken cues from Terraria. But personally, after reaching the depths of the forest caves, it became boring for me. You end up doing the same thing repeatedly—you're forced to revisit previous biomes and delve into the same caves to mine the same ores. This realization dawns on you after the first forest depth cave. It's like the developers ran out of ideas and thought, 'Why not make a SECOND LEVEL OF CAVES? THAT'D BE SO INTERESTING, DAMN.' And you keep delving—first, you mine one ore, make armor, kill a boss. Then they tell you, 'Go to the icy deep cave,' and you go, mine ore again, make armor, kill a boss. Then they tell you again, 'Go to another biome and do the same.' It's mind-blowing gameplay, really, within your grasp.  There's much more to say about this game, like the pet lanterns, of which there's only ONE VARIATION, darn it. After playing this game for 35 hours and defeating the final boss (some Dark Mage), I can say that, at the moment, the game isn't worth the money. These guys just copied and stole all the ideas from Terraria, RimWorld, and possibly even Minecraft. They did what they could and released the game in early access. I genuinely wonder why there are so many positive reviews. If, in the years to come, the devs add more interesting content, revamp the gameplay, maybe ditch the deep caves altogether or just make them more diverse, then perhaps it'll be worth playing. But for now, it all looks very cheap and lazy.

Redacting my original positive review because this game went downhill, IMO. Ever since leaving early access, Necesse has changed in ways that I don't agree with. I actually enjoyed the island-to-island approach mainly for how manageable the caves were. 1.1 merged all the biomes together into this giant sprawling land, meaning you now have a chance of running into an ice cave in the middle of a forest cave, and won't know it's there unless you commit the surface level to memory. Speaking of surface, having every biome clustered together now means running into encounters you're not prepared for. Exploring the land and getting cheap-shotted by a random pirate village is just plain annoying.  But the biggest thing that made me change my mind about this game is how borked the mod support is now. Several talented creators have left the game because each update keeps breaking support for mods. Ordinarily this wouldn't be a problem because Necesse is a good game on it's own, but mods made it better. I'm going to assume that it's an unavoidable good-faith flub on the dev's part and not jump to the conclusion of 'Bethesda level antagonism' just yet.  Regardless, Necesse is still a good game, I personally just don't like the direction it's gone in.

The game promises progression to the likes of Terraria and Rimworld, but so much of this game seems to only replicate the surface-level design of what it inspires itself off of. Everything in this game is recycled from the biomes to the items and equipment.  Each level of weapons/items/trinkets literally just does the exact same thing but with bigger numbers ad nauseum rather than having unique characteristics that make them genuinely interesting. Swords will be swords forever, big swords will be big swords forever, and bows will be bows forever. Unique weapons don't do anything unique (animations, movesets, etc), and even summons are all variations of "thing walks toward enemy and hurts them on contact".  I thought the settlement building would be interesting but ultimately, it's just fluff on top of a simple automation system. There's no micromanagement involved, once you've set them up and set them on their way, they'll do everything on their own. Travel is also made ultimately pointless because you can just travel multiple spaces forward from the edge of an area, traverse back once, and then travel multiple again from the same map edge.  Enemy types are all the same archetypes for the entirety of the game. They're all variants of the zombie, archer, and mage with maybe some kind of fast-moving creature sprinkled in. They all behave exactly the same and attack with whatever basic thing they have equipped (or walk into you, sure). It's just so bland, I really don't have other words for it. The bosses are easily the highlight of the game, but as the game progresses, the boss design gets worse as it starts with genuinely interesting and thoughtful phases that are meant to be learned- whereas later bosses just spam the entire screen with projectiles while flying all over the place. The difficulty of these bosses seems to spike up and down as well, it's not a consistent curve, and most of them don't even have a dedicated boss area (meaning no level design at all)- you just summon them wherever you want in the entire biome.  Maybe it gets more interesting 15 hours in when you finally unlock Essences, I don't know. Once you get the Deep Ladder and see that they intend to just recycle locations after recycling the same enemy types, equipment, and environment hazards over and over, my experience starts to sour. Everything I'd done up to that point feels like it's all just been one huge waste of time as I crawl through tiers of equipment for hours fighting the same recycled enemy-types in the same recycled environments just to get bigger numbers to defeat the next thing the Elder tells me to do.

I am surprised no one else has said it in any review. This is a Terraria clone, through and through. The weapons, armor, enemies, crafting system, settlement system and even some of the npc's are blatant rip off's, not inspiration, rip off. I tried to ignore it at first and told myself "ehhhhh its just a little copying its no big deal." But holy jesus its the entire game. Even the classes are almost all the same. One example that just hit me hard as its one of my fav setts of amor in terraria is the Spider Armor, with the venom and spider staff, its a summoning set, and guess what. Necesse has the exact same armor, with the exact same name, farmed the exact same way, the exact same crafting method, with the exact same mechanics, including the weapons. I haven't checked but it wouldn't surprise me if all these items had the exact same stats. In addiion most of the items are the same and function the same, with as explained before sometimes the exact same name. Even the visual design of crafting stations and items are the same. The only major differences ive seen are: the perspective change and the most of the bosses. Altho some of these bosses look and function in a surprising similar way to bosses that can be found in the Calamity mod for Terraria. Now from the basic research ive done on the dev, I am pretty sure its one person. I do not know if they are a Calamity mod dev but I doubt it. This seems like a person who is trying to make some quick money by copying a very successful game and trying to steal some of that success. Finally, I want to address the reviews of this game. Most of the them say a very common phrase, more or less saying : "This is like Terraria but with a bit of Rimworld." This game is nothing like Rimworld, they do not share mechanics or anything else. But this is definitely like Terraria. So I think i can safely assume, that these reviews are either made by children who barely know what they are talking about, or by the dev as a sort of artificial promotion. Anyways, this game is very suspicious and if I was the Terraria devs I would get a lawyer.  Quick addition, I understand the this game has added a few different things than Terraria, but the sheer amount of "inspiration" is too much to be ignored, this would get you a zero on an assignment. Very unoriginal and disappointing. You could have done something new but you decided to copy on your classmate.

[h1]Necesse, You're Not For Me![/h1] I put about 45 minutes into Necesse. I’ve gotten pretty good over the years at quickly judging a game, based off its [b]Combat, Exploration, and granted Player Freedom[/b], and I find Necesse [i]Sorely Lacking[/i] on these fronts. Also, I plan to refund the game, so I don’t want to go over the 2 hour limit and get stuck with it; so forgive me if I don’t go as long as I normally do here.  Note: I don’t like to make negative, or at least, Not Positive or “thumbs up” reviews, but I think this review could prove useful, and help bring some needed balance to some of the others I have been seeing up here.  Before I get too into this, and to forestall any reflexive fanboys, I will say this: I am aware that Necesse is still considered Early Access; I also know Necesse has been in Early Access since Dec 2019, so, plenty of time has passed to address its numerous issues. It is currently Dec 2023; over 4 years have passed since Necesse was released into seemingly never ending Early Access; so at what point is criticism valid? I say: it is valid immediately, as an “Early Access” launch puts a game up for sale, the moment that happens, critique is fair game. A launch, is still, a launch.  Anyway, I had looked forward to playing, then showing some Necesse gameplay, but I just can’t, as it’s just not very good sadly. :( I was bored out of my mind playing this watered down, super slow, not MineCraft / Terraria, and [b]watered down and slow I think is the perfect description of Necesse.[/b]  [h1]Like Terraria, but Worse![/h1] I didn’t much like Terraria, I found its combat very lacking, and its flat, 2D animations terrible, but somehow, [b]Necesse has managed to take the terrible animations of Terraria, and make an even worse combat system![/b] That’s some kind of accomplishment, but not a good one!  [u]At least Terraria let me dig where I want, and did not deny me the power of Z lvl traversal on my terms![/u] That’s right, no digging down as you want in Necesse; it seems all you have is a dungeon lvl to descend if you can find one, or make a ladder, and go do some horizontal mining underground: YUCK! [b]That joy of exploration your own way is utterly lacking in Necesse[/b], and just removes so much of what makes these kind of open world, exploration focused game fun, and Necesse is much the lesser for lacking it. :(   [h1]The Combat in Necesse is Flat Out Terrible![/h1] That alone is reason for me to down vote this one! Combat is such a Core Pillar of the gameplay here, and to botch it so utterly, I simply cannot overlook it. I know that [i]eventually[/i], you can get a trinket, (bound off space by default), that grants some mysterious ability (likely a dodge or block), and [i]eventually[/i], you can get an armor ability as well. [b]My question is: Why are such Vital and important combat features so tucked away and hidden from the early gameplay? Why do you get them so late?[/b] Why are they so absent from the initial game, where players will be assessing your game to see if it’s worth their time? This is a [i]Terrible[/i] way of structuring your combat progression; which tells me that combat was clearly NOT a major focus in development; which is downright shocking considering how common it is!    Here’s the thing: I like MineCraft (especially modded Minecraft) I like Wayward, I love Vintage Story, so why don’t I like Necesse? I was fairly certain I would, but I don’t. Not, one, bit! I love exploration and seeing new things, I like interesting combat, and I like RPG mechanism and player choice, so why don’t I like Necesse? The problem is: [b]Necesse is so Slow, and so Restrictive via its little island design, that Exploration rapidly becomes a Deeply Slow, Deeply Frustrating Experience.[/b]  So, right at the start, you’re stuck on baby island, with no seeming danger during the day or at night. There is an old man who pesters you, and lives in your house for some reason, trying to get rid of him resulted in him killing me, as old dude has super high stats; [i]I guess he was able to put in some attributes on character creation, I only got to pick an outfit and a name; lame![/i] [b]So, the game is already making My Character Feel Weak and Useless, right at the start; Not Exactly the Best Way to Introduce an RPG![/b]  Anyway, already thoroughly sick of old man island, and already getting bored waiting for the promised reavers and raiders, that are supposed to attack at some point, I get tired of waiting, and set off. [b]I am NOT the type to sit around and wait for the danger, I go and meet it head on![/b]✊  So, I slowly waded (yes, I literally waded or doggy paddled over to another whole island; Wayward is dying of embarrassment), over to an adjacent island, hoping to find something, anything, more interesting except for yet more for more cows and sheep, and blueberries. I finally got there at night, and there were some brain dead zombies to fight. [b]The combat was Absolutely Terrible[/b] by the way, just spam sword attack; it forces them back and kills them. No experience, of course, and I don’t remember getting anything off the rotten meat bags; just more disappointment. :( I stop caring and throw, letting them port me back to my island on death.  I had turned on full loot drop on death, to add some kind of tension to the game, yet I felt ZERO drive to go get my stuff; I just didn’t care. :( That is TERRIBLE! [u]If your game is just so bland, so lacking, that the player does NOT care what happens to their character, than you have failed to make that character meaningful to the player.[/u]  Anyway, please let me know if anyone thinks I’m off base with any of this, or if anyone thinks I am being unfair, but I don’t think that I am. This feels like something that needs to be said, and is not being said enough.  I should note, I read some of these positive reviews, and initially, I just didn’t understand them. I read some of them again, trying to discover what exactly about this super slow, super grind fest that interests them; and it is this: they are grinders. I am not. I like exploration. I like RPGs, and I like combat, and I will tolerate grinding in the pursuit of strengthening a character, or enjoying tactical, engaging combat, but none of that is present here, thus the naked grind so present in Necesse disgusts and repels me. The super slow, plodding pace here [i]Nauseates[/i] me.  I know you can get a mount, [i]eventually[/i], but maybe, just maybe, the movement should not be so disgustingly slow, and maybe a positive incentive, as opposed to the current negative incentive of avoiding the super slow speed, should be used to encourage the player to keep [strike]grinding[/strike] playing.    So I suppose, unless you are a grinder, stay far away from Necesse, as it will very likely deeply disappoint you. Sorry for the bad news, but it is much better than regretting your time spend in a bad game; that, you cannot get back. :(

It's not that the game is lacking content, but it's lacking dimension. None of the mechanics are any fun or have depth, and it would take a complete overhaul for me to enjoy it. It just feels like a continuous slog to get nowhere and I think the people comparing the game to Terraria, Stardew, or Rimworld have never played them, as their gameplay loops are far superior.  Movement and combat are clunky, and extremely lacking when compared to Terraria and even Minecraft. It's like a settlement manager with content locked behind bosses, which aren't fun to fight. They have high HP and high damage, and are very one-dimensional with their attacks. It's not particularly difficult to fight them, but it takes a long time, and it's a nuisance because you deal no damage with each hit. I've never felt "strong" in this game, even with lategame gear killing NPCs, raiders, and slightly earlier bosses still takes active gameplay.   The settlement management is convenient, it saves a lot of time when you can get NPCs to farm basic resources, and then eventually send them to collect specific items for you. But so many of the NPCs perform the exact same tasks, with most of the difference being the items you can purchase and sell, which are lacking in utility.  There are too many items, a lot of things are there just as padding. Monsters drop items, but there's no purpose to them. When you get to the next biome you pick up the new stuff and discard your old items, items like trinkets you have to discover in chests, rather than crafting. When I started playing, there was so much gunk to toss out every time I went spelunking; walking around gets you so many items between the worms, grass seeds, flowers, random fauna, and enemies that spawn. Eventually it gets better because the NPCs can auto-manage chests, and inventory management upgrades are available as you defeat bosses, but gating them into the late game is unfun in my opinion.  Progression is too linear and all the quests are the same, you fight your caves, then the biomes, then the deep caves, then the deep biomes in the same order. The quests are just to kill X monster from that biome, then the biome boss. And each boss is in a different biome, so if you want to fight them optimally, you have to create an arena every time. Most of the monsters are reskins and don't make thematic sense. For example, the late game biomes have jackal enemies chunking me for half my health. I understand a giant worm or vampire chunking me, but a regular jackal? All the enemy AI is one-dimensional as well, they just run at you to deal contact damage.   There's a lack of cohesion between the game when travelling between these islands because it's literally a teleport. Infinite generation but no incentive to explore, since all the islands look the same anyways. And on the topic of appearance, it's not the prettiest looking game.  The developer recently added a mana system, but why? Just so I waste my inventory slots on mana potions and mana regeneration modifiers?

Necesse tries to imitate Terraria, but doesn't execute any aspect of game design as successfully as the original. As a result, Necesse is essentially a slightly worse version of Terraria with all the same disadvantages in boss fights, but without the charm or polish of the original.

Necesse is a town builder with task automation for your NPC villagers, but progression is gated behind bullet-hell bosses.  I do not recommend this game for anyone who (like me) dislikes bullet hells.  The first boss is above ground, so you can chop down enough trees for a fighting arena.  The next 3 bosses are underground.  One of them has a sizable arena.  The other two require you to strain your tendons to mine out an arena in which to battle them (assuming you don't accidentally trigger the fight early with a stray bullet).  And if you do die during the fight, the boss escapes, and you have to farm up another summoning item.  One of them just stays there until you kill him, but he happens to be behind a really long corpse run.  So tedious.    I have spent way too much time travelling.

In its current state, I can't really recommend the game as it is  ... very basic. The basics are good and the game has a lot of potential, however, in comparison to Terraria, RimWorld or Stardew Valley, it lacks advanced game mechanics, content and the challenge.   - The settlers are one-dimensional lacking any kind of personality and don't interact with other settlers. Except for their skills (which are mostly similar for every settler), there is no reason to take them in.  - Your village will get raided, however, the raiders are not really a danger to your village as they get crushed fast after you're moderately equipped and have some kind of ranged weapon. You don't even need any of the settlers to defend your village. - You can travel to different islands that vary in their biome and may contain a village with settlers and a dungeon entrance. However, islands don't have much to offer despite a few sheeps and cows, and maybe some zombies that appear in the night. There is not much stuff to discover on islands. - One island is just like the other and there is not really an incentive to discover new islands after you have found an island of every biome. - As I said, islands with villages contain a dungeon entrance. Those dungeons are mostly there for farming loot and mining ores. Those dungeons are pretty huge but don't offer much content-wise which is why it doesn't make much sense to explore the entire dungeon. Keep in mind that there are countless islands with dungeons that are basically the same dungeon you already explored on another island. - Fishing is boring, melee is pretty much worthless - Farming is just bland. There is no reason to grow different crops. You can just grow one kind of crop which will be sufficient as settlers don't have any preference or aversions that could influence their happiness. Yes, a varied diet will increase their happiness but ... - Happiness of settlers doesn't matter anyway as it only influences shop prices. Settlers may strike if they are unhappy, but it is not a challenge to keep them happy. Most of the stuff in the shop you can craft yourself anyway or get in dungeons or from boss drops. - what the f is the purpose of those quests?  Every major game mechanic (exploration, fighting, colony sim, farming, rpg) is just bland and needs to be fleshed out. However, the game is only developed by one guy which is why expectations should be lowered in this regard. It should also be mentioned, that this game supports modding but I haven't looked into it yet.

I previously had a thumbs-up review until 1.0 took away the game I signed up for.  Previously, Necesse's game flow was a unique, if a bit clumsy, island system. You would sail away from an island and it'd open a global map to pick another neighboring island to go to, so your exploration is targeted and intentional. Also, once you put a settlement on an island, the entire island was the settlement, so you could build around its geography without having to worry about the exact placement of bounds or upgrading your settlement size or any of that ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥.  This island system was less than perfect, of course. I was expecting it to, you know, be revised.  Not ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ removed.  With 1.0, the game has abolished the one and only unique element it had and adopted the generic procedurally-generated seamless world of yesterdecade to go with its generic, something-else-does-it-better everything else. It's now a pain in the ass to find anywhere or go anywhere, and settlements are an area around the spot you put the flag on, which are also upgraded in size later, making it nearly impossible to line up so you can pick the ideal spot to build around.  There was never anything else about this game to write home about. There's never anything else about this [i]entire genre[/i] to write home about. Sandbox games always have minimal story, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ combat based entirely on number beeg, some horrible chore of a food system or another, and a braindead dodge or block mechanic, and migraine-inducing inventory management & crafting. The only two things about this game that were good, exploration and building, were just both nuked.  Supposedly the Necesse community really wanted this change and I just never knew about this. Why not, you ask? Why the ♥♥♥♥ would I. I proactively never give fans of anything the time of day and neither should you. "Fans" are not fans. They're tourists. They're ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ with ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ taste who destructively consume and leave all our culture & art a barren wasteland like a plague of locusts. If they have enough money they become stockholders and destroy companies more directly. That's one of several reasons why big companies almost completely ignore feedback. But Necesse is an indie game, it's made by one person who literally can't do that and may be too naive to know you should. So Necesse is yet another casualty.  Yes, you eventually unlock some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ teleport stone system. This was also present in the island map system. I never used it. A teleport system's purpose is to skip parts of the game that are not fun. Well guess what, that is now the entire game. So I'm skipping the entire game, and I don't need a teleport stone to do that.

On the surface it's a pretty good and fun game, however as someone who has played Rimworld, Minecraft and Terraria, this game is so heavily inspired by these games, you can almost say that the exact systems were almost copied 1 to 1 without much attempt at putting their own creative spin on it. (this is based on my last playthrough from a few months ago) After about 5h of gameplay it's pretty obvious that the developer decided to copy a bunch of mechanics from games he likes, without really understanding why those mechanics worked so well in their respective games.  The colony management side of the game feels clunky and unnecessary, I could not get invested in it despite being a huge Rimworld and Kenshi fan.  Farming was... ok-ish?  The combat is plain awful, you character has no mobility whatsoever especially when you compare them with the mobs and bosses the game pits you against, like, the bosses say Bullet hell but your movement says the complete opposite.  Traveling sucks, the game has this grid map where you can see all the other islands, because yes, every biome is a little island that you gotta swim or sail to to actually progress (with a loading screen too, since the transition is not seamless)  Progression boiled down to: Find new biome, go underground, kill first underwhelming boss, mine new ore, find and kill second underwhelming boss, progresss to next biome and repeat. I wanna mention how underwhelming, frustating and unsatisfying the combat is again since it deeply affects the progression  Graphics are... not amazing, really. I'm a fan of pixelated games, but this game has some pretty bad art overall. The magic items especially just look boring.  Speaking of items, most of them are straight up shameless copies of other items from terraria and the likes, which is hilarious, but also quite disappointing. If I wanted to play with those items I would just... play terraria.  Those are the main negatives I recall having a problem with when I last played, I'll probably update this if I ever get the opportunity to play this again, but I'm not counting on that.

1.0 EDIT: I will have to sadly change my review to not recommended. Dont get me wrong, the core game is good, and i was really hyped about this when i bought it years ago as beta, leaving it in my backlog to get back to it once content has been added, but i must say... over the last i dont even know how many years, woefully little has been done that i can see.  The problems that were around with the beta are still all here: Extreme lack of items, biomes, enemies, bosses. The game is aiming for terraria style exploration, survival and crafting, but has 1/10th to 1/20th of the area, diversity, items, biomes, enemies, etc.  The graphic was updated and upgraded and an incursion system has been added, and thats about it for the last 3 years of development. Sorry, this is way too little. You can clear it on normal in less than 10 hours. Great potential, f-ing disgraceful "i got bored of developing it" or "i never had enough time/ppl/resources to work on it" execution.  ********************************************************************************  If you liked terraria, you will like Necesse. Extremely similar titles aside from one being a side scroller and the other being topdown. Handles the concept of exploration way better than terraria does, but (albeit still in early access) exactly because of that, it dreadfully needs more biomes, events and curiosities.   TL;DR: Pros: - Terraria in topdown mode - All the good stuff with equipments, crafting, enchanting, summoning, magic, ranged, melee - Town building instead of base building, with npcs that can automatize processes and item sorting, outdoing terraria's "do nothing npc vendor storage" approach, by lightyears. - Exploration to other isles with different biomes - Plenty of bosses - Coop - Fun  Cons: - Biomes, events and everything that is exploration, could use a LOT more expansion and diversity - Town building is... well, its perfect in terms of automation. The AI does well with its tasks and there are a diverse number of npcs with different skills and purposes, but it would be nice if your town felt a bit more lively, like with npcs doing random stuff, engaging eachother, forming relationships potentially  I'll be honest, pretty much the only problem i am having with the game currently is that i want more. You have a basically endlessly explorable map, but just 4 repeating biomes for it. That is the current largest issue of the game atm, imo.  The game has a large amount of potential, and even if its done in the form of dlcs, i'd happily pay for expanding it. Its worth it.

As a gaming enthusiast, I eagerly anticipated the release of Necesse. The game promised an immersive experience filled with stunning visuals, an intriguing storyline, and a unique gameplay style.  Unfortunately, my excitement quickly waned as I encountered a significant flaw in the game. The most basic WASD movement, an essential feature for navigating any virtual world, is virtually non-functional. I initially thought this was a temporary glitch, so I restarted the game, only to find that the problem persisted.  This lack of basic movement functionality is both puzzling and frustrating. A game with such intricate design and captivating visuals should provide a seamless gaming experience, allowing players to fully immerse themselves in the world. The inability to move around hampers exploration and diminishes the overall enjoyment of the game.  In conclusion, Necesse is an artistically impressive game with a captivating visual design and a fitting soundtrack. Unfortunately, the game's beauty is marred by a glaring issue with the basic WASD movement mechanics. Until this problem is resolved, it is difficult to fully enjoy and appreciate the immersive world of Necesse. It is my hope that the developers will promptly address this issue, allowing players to experience the game as it was intended.

Note: This review was submitted before the most recent content update. I have not felt the urge to play this again, but based on the patch notes it looks like some of the issues below may have been addressed. I stand by the original review based on what I played, but I felt like it would be unfair to leave the review without mentioning the changes. I will redo the review when I get some more hours in.  I wasn't sure what to think about this game at first. Now that I've put in too much time I can safely stay that it wasn't really worth it (funny because there is an achievement that seems to agree). It is very clearly inspired by the likes of Terraria and Rimworld, but it borrows so much that it doesn't seem to have room for many improvements. Also, I personally think it's a massive red flag for any early access game to allow workshop submissions.[list][/list][list][hr][/hr] [/list][list][/list] I got this game to play multiplayer with a friend. The multiplayer seems solid from what I've experienced, but as a warning, characters are locked to the generated world, not to a profile like many other games like this are. I thought I would be able to craft some starting stuff and hop onto a friends world, but was met with a character creation prompt when joining them. I honestly like this, but keep that in mind if you want to play with friends.[list][/list][list][hr][/hr] [/list][list][/list] The settlement system seems to be very similar to parts of Rimworld. The person I play with has tons of time with that game so they were able to get things started easily. As someone who has only seen Rimworld played I had a hard time doing anything but the most basic things and eventually gave up. For example, I tried to get settlers to use a cooking station and set it to "make grilled rabbit until X" set X to 5 and went dungeon diving. I came back to every settler having 43 cooked rabbit legs. So now they all just eat simple food and it seems to have had no bearing on their happiness, the only metric that settlers currently have. The people you can recruit can also do most jobs, so unique types don't really feel that unique outside of say a fisherman. I don't doubt the systems in place are powerful if you can figure them out, but I just found that getting a functional farm running and slapping beds in houses was really all I needed. [list][/list][list][hr][/hr] [/list][list][/list]My biggest gripe with the game is that the movement is just way too slow. Islands are very small, so I assume the low movement speed is to make the world feel bigger. The game has a unique 'usable' slot for one of your trinkets and very quickly gives you the dash item you can see used frequently in one of the trailers. Generally in games, mobility/movement speed increases are more valuable than other options because the utility given is not conditional. It is always good to be able to dash. Having a unique active slot is great, but I slotted in the dash item and never looked back. I've explored all available biomes and defeated most of the bosses, but nothing I've seen comes close to being as good. This segues into my next big problem.[list][/list][list][hr][/hr] [/list][list][/list]Melee is objectively bad, especially for bosses. This is a problem in many games to be fair. If you can hit something from far away without risking damage yourself, that is probably the best option. It also doesn't help that the damage instances in the game happen very quickly (multiple times a second) and touching a monster will hurt you. Combine this with the fact that you take a massive movement penalty when attacking, the fact that shields occupy the same slot as the dash, and with the slow base movement melee becomes unusable for bosses and dangerous otherwise. There are health potions and some food/buffs to help offset this, but the 30 second reuse timer on potions means you need to prioritize not being hit over doing damage. I also am surprised that this game has no real form of character progression like levels or skill trees, which I feel like would help address some of these issues. [list][/list][list][hr][/hr] [/list][list][/list] There are no healing options other than health/regen potions and passive buffs from some foods. You also can't stack multiple food buffs. I think this is a bad take in games like this, but I understand the difficulties this presents to a developer. That said, I found many of the bosses too easy and rather uninspired design wise. Bosses don't really start to feel like fights until towards the end of the currently available content. The auto-tracking bosses use is fairly easy to feel out and manipulate.[list][/list][list][hr][/hr] [/list][list][/list]Dungeon diving is probably the best part of the game, but it gets very stale. Seeds are often the best thing you can hope to find as new seeds often lead to a bunch of new recipes. I think this is great, but after the second/third biome I felt like I stopped getting interesting drops and started just getting seeds. Bosses seem to have a lot of the best loot, which is fine and kind of makes sense. But I feel like I stopped being excited about what I would find exploring. The loop is basically; do a dungeon area, get the new ore, make a pickaxe/gear, kill the boss a few times, on to the next biome. These areas kind of feeling empty is likely because the game is in early access and I'm sure it will be expanded. But it just started to feel very samey until the end of current content.[list][/list][list][hr][/hr] [/list][list][/list]Overall, it's been fun to play with others and I'll likely still play with others but I don't think this game has enough to warrant picking up as it is right now. Maybe I've just been spoiled by my other recent early access experiences, but I feel like this game is either going to take a while to get where it needs to or it will just rely on its mod community to make it more enjoyable. [list][/list][list][hr][/hr] [/list][list][/list]Fun with friends, but maybe pass for now.

I love games like RimWorld, Minecraft, No Man's Sky, Starbound, CoreKeeper... games with mining, crating, combat, colonies, base-building, farming, random terrain, and treasures to loot.   Necesse should be a game I love... but...  I bought Necesse in March 2021. It's November of 2022, at the time I'm writing this. It's taken me this entire time to really get into the game. Why?   The sound and sprite design.   To start the game off, we have a chipper toon-y sounding menu theme. The music fits fairly well. It's upbeat and adventurous, but also not memorable at all. For a menu screen, I give it a pass.   Then, we have our overworld theme. It's sort of a jazzy, cool, mysterious ambient theme. Compared to the vibrant and expansive overworld, the music feels like a direct clash. The overworld theme gives off the ambience of a casino or maybe a dungeon. It's not at all a bright or ear-catching theme. It's not engaging. It's just this... flat, not-memorable, ambience.   For the place we're meant to explore and build a base on, I expect to hear a more robust, varied, and vivid tune that will catch my attention and carry me along my worldly expeditions.  Necess' overworld theme does not do that. It's too dreary and dull. Again, it could maybe be used in a dungeon or an NPC pub.   But I'd enjoy Necesse much more if the overworld had a more tantalizing and energizing theme to go alongside it.   Our overworld night theme is a little bit better. It has a sort of spooky, sad, and mysterious melody that carries a vague sense of urgency. It's better, but it still feels like the kind of music you'd find in RPGMaker as the default pack. There's no real life and vibrancy to it. Nothing to give me some incentive to go out and fight mobs.  Overall, it's just very DULL.  The underground/caving theme feels much the same. It doesn't match the ambience, it doesn't stand out, it doesn't offer anything catchy to carry me on an adventure. It's bland and could be replaced with any of the above songs without notice.   The graphics are my other big beef. However, this is isolated mostly to the player/character graphics as well as the janky grid system.   The characters you choose from are very... masculine-leaning. No matter how hard I've tried to make a cute girl character, I always end up with an boxy man-shape. There's just no diverse customization for the characters at all. This could be rememdied in the later game with some cool helmets or particle effects (Terraria's paint system). But to start off with, I find the player characters just very... stiff, unvaried, and manly. Kind of like Minecraft villagers.  I want to be able to make cute girly characters!   The rest of the graphics are very mediocre. I can see it being a style choice. And, for the overworld, underground, and NPC's, it's not bad. It doesn't stand out and feel pretty, but it's also passable.   The biggest graphical issue comes with mining! I can never tell where my pickaxe is really hitting due to the stone "tiles" not aligning correctly with the overhead lighting grid! I end up regularly mining the wrong thing just because of this misalignment.   Necesse seems like a fun game for the mechanics, alone. This is why I've kept playing as long as I have. I've heard that the combat gets very fun and so does the rest of the game. However, while I loosely "recommend" it, the lack-luster music AND graphics make Necesse a game that I wouldn't rave about to my friends. If it was just one or the other, it would be more playable. But I'm finding the game very difficult to get into as it stands, now.  I debated making this review for fear of making the dev feel hurt or disappointed. As a creator, myself, I know how difficult it is to receive feedback.  I think Necesse has a LOT of potential! It mostly just needs a good polish over the sounds. Maybe the grid to be aligned better.  Necesse is not at all a bad game. But, it really drove home the point to me just how much the audio design carries a game. Games like Undertale, Minecraft, and Terraria are as popular as they are, in part, to their killer soundtracks! I think Necesse could rise up there, too. Just needs some tweaking, first.   Since the game is early access, still, I have hope that the dev will read this feedback and continue to expand the game. In the meantime, if you're considering buying it, I'd recommend putting some YouTube on in the background. Or just muting the music entirely
