The only reason why I'm leaving a negative review is because I'm baffled how this game got overwhelmingly positive reviews. Don't get me wrong, it's nice but that's all. It feels like an extended tech demo so the price is fair, but 3.4k reviews with o. pos. rating is too generous.   Feels very dull & when I play this I feel like I'm playing Terraria without the fun factor. It's like all the pieces are here, but that magic that makes the game fun is not.  EDIT: Considering some people decided to spent some points on rewards under this review it made me realize it doesn't quite sound right, so let me clarify what I meant by it (and also let me enable the comments cause they are disabled by default).   When I see game having overwhelmingly positive rating I see that its playerbase thinks the game is basically 10/10. Barely any game on Steam gets that kind of rating, one of the prime & valid examples is previously mentioned Terraria that has more than enough reasons to justify its rating.  When I played this game it was average to the bone. It's like I've played this stuff thousand times already - you got your caves and biomes, all your ores to smelt, all your gear to craft, there's no difference comparing to other games like this (all the ores are also literally the same). As it is right now, the colony system is very basic and for me it isn't enough of a twist to fool my brain into thinking it's something more than another generic explore/craft/explore game.   Does that mean the game is bad and has no potential? Not at all. As I said, this game is like a definition of average for me. I'd give it 5 or 6 out of 10 if I had to rate it, so in terms of review I wish I could leave a NEUTRAL one. Obviously Steam has no choice like that.  So why negative instead of none? Well this is what connects us back to my original review. I believe that this game has too generous rating and this fact completely justifies me giving it a negative review.   Would I give it neutral if I had a choice? Absolutely. But I don't, and we all would be lying if we'd say that Review Rating isn't a feature that at least slightly influences our purchases. For me, when I see overwhelmingly positive rating on a game, which is almost impossible of a task, I expect a polished, refined & (especially) fun game.  So I bought it, and got none of that. My impression was [b]mixed[/b].

This isn't a bad game, it's just not what I hoped for and thus not for me.  The exploration isn't what I was expecting from the description.   Minecraft/Terraria-like with town building and procedural exploration?   Sign me up!  But the proc gen exploration isn't very good at all.    Every area is a small island.   There are grass islands, snow islands, desert islands, etc, but everything is a small island surrounded entirely by water.   To move to another biome, you get into the water, sail to the edge of the screen, and are hit with a fast travel screen to get to the next area after a brief load.   It's not seamless, and unfortunately it's just ultimately really uninteresting to me and does not give the sense of exploration I wanted at all.   I could deal with the load screens if the biomes were contiguous like on Minecraft, but there's no thrill of exploration for me when it's all small disconnected islands forever.

Edit 5/18/26: My original review was in September of 2022 back before they had the infinite world generation. If I never knew about this game and had finally tried it these days then I'm sure I'd absolutely love it, but I'm keeping my review the same as a reminder of what this once was. Necesse broke my heart back then... And I may never come back... I don't know if I can forgive...   ---------------------------------------------  I actually loved this game for a bit, the city-builder aspect of it was awesome, but in the end the map setup just got to me. The map is made up of a bunch of very small islands or "instances" that you don't actually boat to, you ride your boat away from shore then a map pops up where you can choose which island to teleport to next. A more seamless and massive map like Rimworld would be much more ideal and would add endless hours of gameplay. The transitions just take everything away from the game, really they shouldn't even have a boat, the map should just pop up when you reach the shore and save you some time.  All the islands are terribly small and just got too boring trying to travel - you fully explore each island within ten minutes (again, these maps are TINY), destroy all the mobs, and then go into the cave to defeat the boss. Rinse and repeat, go to the next island and do the exact same thing. Once I realized this was the concept I gave up on it. I was looking for a Rimworld-esque game where you control one main character and just make choices for all the others, which is where this game did good but again I just hate the map transitioning system, complete buzzkill and breaks the immersion. Swipe.

TL;DR: This game is not bad. It has potential. It's clear a lot of love went into it. However, it does not deserve overwhelmingly positive reviews. It is priced correctly for its current content. I'd pay way more if it delivered on its implicit promises. My advice: wait for developer to polish and add depth.  I'll echo what other reviewers have said before. The game tries to be a blend of Terraria (spelunking, combat style) and Rimworld (colony management). However, it totally misses what makes Terraria fun (bunch of items to make spelunking and combat enjoyable) and what makes Rimworld challenging (having to meet the needs of your colonists; having to deal with scarcity). Instead, the game makes you rely on dodging bosses without good ways to dodge, the spelunking is an absolute grind to get through the *constantly* respawning enemies, and the colonists don't need anything to function while filling up your chests with tons and tons of farmed items... that you have no use for.  Yeah, I'll play the game for a few more hours with a buddy of mine, sure. But beyond that, it needs some serious QoL, more engaging progression, a deeper colonist system, and any form of meaningful colony progression before I'd actually recommend this to anyone.   And don't get me wrong- I'd happily pay 3x the current price if the game delivers on its promise of blending colony management and dungeon crawling. But right now, it knows it can't deliver on that promise, hence the price tag.  Good luck to the dev on finishing what they started.

We, a group i'd consider veterans and huge fans of the involved genres, did not enjoy our playthrough of Necesse at all. It is not a bad game and shows a lot of potential, but certainly needs a lot of polish to become decently enjoyable. The game attempts to bring together a combat and progression oriented experience, similar to games like Terraria, with village management, similar to games like Rimworld.  So why did we not like it? While we did not dive too deep into the village management aspects, those seem to work pretty well. The combat and progression on the other hand got us frustrated really quickly.  There is several factors involved in this, which i will try to address below.  [b]Slow:[/b] Throughout the game we always felt as if we were simply slow. Not just "too slow" for anything in particular. Simply unenjoyably slow, even after collecting and stacking accessories that give you some kind of speed improvement. There is even some items that are supposedly too powerful to use in combat, thus only adjust speed out-of-combat.. yet you still feel as if you are crawling through the world.  [b]Normal Combat:[/b] The normal combat and exploration experience is decent. Nothing too polished, or fancy, but it works out for early access! Balance wise however, you can basically craft summoner gear and wont have to worry about most normal enemies ever again, if you so desire.  [b]Bosses:[/b] The bossfights were what finally drove us away from the game, as they are simply not fun. At least not for us. As usual in this type of game your typical boss moves around a lot and loves to simply walk over you. Normally these bosses have certain patterns, which allow you to get some damage in, independant of what weapon variant you use to fight the boss. While there usually is a "best tool for the job" for this kind of boss design, in Necesse it feels like anthing but the intended way to fight the boss is actively mocked. We found this annoying to say the least, because you usually dont craft several sets of armor at each stage of the game (Melee, Range, Magic, Summoner). Most bosses generally move faster than summoned minions, so for the most part those are pretty useless at actually getting in decent DPS on bosses. So you just use another weapon, right? Yeah, as long as it's not a melee weapon, for most bosses. They are all faster than you. They wont stop long enough to get damage in - or even for you to arrive at their new location.  There are several, more general, problems we had with bossfights as well. Compared to similar games, Armor does not appear to do a whole lot. This, and similar issues with weapons, make progression feel a bit unrewarding. Meaning you will take pretty chunky hits. Usually this is fine, as you are supposed to dodge most attacks. However, we found the options to do so pretty limited. There is an item that grants you a small dash. There is even an accessory to add two additional charges to it. Certain bosses love to do a pattern of quick charges at you. And after you used up your dashes, they are on cooldown. This being the only meaningful way of actually dodging, we found the overall experience to be lackluster. The developer nicely responded to my previous, more ranty, review and confirmed that dodging is the intended way to fight bosses, and that he has no problems doing so. Which is fine, maybe we all just equally suck at Necesse, despite having played this type of game for hundreds of hours previously. I would however argue that you can learn to deal with (or even predict) any and every kind of boss pattern, no matter how limited or unfun the tools given to overcome the challenge are. That does not make it a good experience or design choice. This is however subjective and everybody will like or dislike different design choices.  No matter your stance on dodging bosses in this game however.. you cant dodge, what you cant see coming. A certain deeper boss basically vanishes after every move, which is pretty annoying all on its own. He however also loved to simply reappear [b]inside[/b] one of us, dealing a large chunk of damage by doing so. Not sure if this is intended, a bug, or simply a coop issue, but it happened way too frequently and was way too punishing to be enjoyable.   We had other minor issues with the game. Like the above-average inventory management issues, the clunky inventory itself, how most items are made through one workbench, without a search function, or the unusual travel / map / world design itself.  However, those are mostly easy to improve, for example by having earlier access to some of the inventory management pouches.  If i had to describe our experience in one word, it would be: frustrating. Necesse is one of those rare cases where i simply cannot grasp the excellent steam review score: 95% at the time of writing. As i mentioned above, it is certainly not a bad game, but i personally can not comprehend how noone else would get frustrated by some of the issues highlighted above. All in all, i look forward to checking out the game in a couple more years, but for now i cannot recommend playing it.

Graphics don't make the game fun. It's not that the graphics aren't good enough. It's more that the _style_ of the graphics is off-putting to me.  Gathering was clunky (I.e. difficulty shouldn't be from walking up just right and clicking on just the right spot in an isometric game.)  Glad others like it, but I'll give it a pass.

Truthfully, this game IS a combination of those games, if those games had 1/10th of the content they actually have.  Necesse does everything right, there's just not enough of it. In 2 days my buddy and I managed to beat the game. It's a game that has you dying to keep playing it, but then leaves you completely deflated once you realize you've already seen all there is to see in such a short time.  For instance, yes, you can automate your farms and your settlements. But for what purpose? Why do I need to craft sugary drinks and quality food when I can beat the game without it? Why do I need to farm 100s of steaks? It doesn't get me anything. There's no point. There is no "end-game" in Necesse. One minute you're working for it, the next thing you know it's all over.  P.S. The combat trailer for the game shows you everything there is to see as far as combat goes. All the bosses etc. There's nothing beyond that combat trailer to see.

For the first 7-8 hours I thought the game was great, however my interest in playing began to diminish enormously as soon as I started doing boss fights. The first one wasn't too bad, but it goes rapidly downhill from there.  The second boss I attempted was the Spider Queen, who just glitches through walls. The next boss I attempted was the Void Wizard, which is just a bullet hell nightmare. Not to mention he can turn on a dime while firing off his spells which makes melee impossible as even with full frost shard armour and 230 hps he completely decimates your health in one volley.  Which brings me on to my next complaint: I ran back to see if I could finish him off with ranged, aaaaand of course he'd reset back to full health. All that time and effort spent getting to the fight itself twice completely wasted as none of the hundreds of enemies I had to kill to get there drop any items whatsoever, and there is no XP in the game, no levels, no stats, no skills, nothing. Which begs the question what's the point? I might as well just run past all the enemies straight to the boss fight. Very, very poor game design.   My next issue is gear upgrades feel pointless.  When I upgraded to copper armour I didn't feel like it was any improvement over leather, when I upgraded to iron armour the same, and so I thought I'll get a full set of frost shard armour and weapons, then I'll be fine! Nope, it's just as useless.  This is made worse by the fact that health potions are on something daft like a 30 second cooldown, and only restore 50hps. Great, so for boss fights they are useless too.  It's a real shame as for the first 8 hours or so I was really enjoying the game, but the boss fights and game balance issues have pretty much killed my interest in continuing.

My conclusion of this games recommendation will change after the development of this title.  Firstly I'd like to clarify that after contacting the developer and receiving very passionate and swift response to my concerns... he shows great promise for this industry and he's absolutely earned my admiration for the time and attention he's devoted to the creation of this game.  However i need to specify that if anybody is looking to INVEST in an alternative "Terraria" inspired experience, this is a great game to lean towards - the entry price is low and the product received greatly exceeds the price tag. However i definitely want to clarify a few vitally important aspects to consider with your expectations.  Unfortunately this game feels obsolete at the current moment in time. If anybody is also reminiscent of the early alpha days of "terraria" the title itself was very bare bones but highly enjoyable, this game feels identical to that, its not quite had the time to develop its own unique identity and feels far to familar to a title i played back in 2012.  Furthermore, while the lack of depth you'll receive from this title is justifiable (early product of a single dev) unfortunately that doesnt remedy the fact that this game (in its current state) feels like an absolutely fantastic foundation to a game that already exists and its heartbreaking. The moment this game has an opportunity to expand its content and develop its own identity by approaching systems very differently (such as a leveling system based on the distance from the home tile or a feature that seperates itself from other titles such as terraria.) I cant see many reasons to encourage an individual to play this instead of terraria.   I'm eagerly watching this game on a low priority filter - Id suggest encouraging more great work from the developer through the purchasing of this game, however i seriously feel there needs to be that substansial "2.0" update that diversifies its content and opens more opportunities to unique experiences.  Kindest Regards Matt

Before you read any further, i want you to remember the following:  [h1] I love Terraria, i love Rimworld, and i really wanted to like this game. [/h1]  [b] ... but sadly, this game doesnt pull of either of those game mechanics and still doesnt accomplish being its own thing. It is flawed, and i couldnt bring myself to play this game any longer. [/b]  First things first: If you are the developer and reading this, dont feel discouraged. Keep developing and adding things to the game, trust me, i would love to come back one day.  [hr][/hr]  [h1] With that out of the way: [/h1]  I liked the premise of Necesse, it seemed like a fun little twist between terraria and rimworld. So i bought it without giving it much of a thought.  [h1] The Looks: [/h1]  When i first started up the game i was quite surprised about how ugly the game looks. I know, the trailers dont look that bad but it really looks worse in person. The whole color scheme looks like something straight out of MS Paint. It lacks personality and is devoid of any color gradations. It just doesnt look like something that much thought was put into. I know, it is only one developer right now, but if you lack the skills in that department, simply outsource it.  The funny thing is, that as soon as you enter the dungeon, it gets slightly better. It doesnt look as bad anymore and is acceptable. But still: Being in the overworld hurt my eyes. No actually, i got dizzy after playing a while.  [h1] The UI: [/h1]  The UI is extremely rough. Straight out of Vanilla Minecraft, and somehow it manages to look even worse. As i said, it lacks personality. It is clunky, confusing at best, but hey, its EA right now so maybe this is going to change in the future.  [h1] The Sound Design: [/h1]  Again, straight out of Vanilla Minecraft. I know that its much to demand from a single developer, but yet again, outsource it.  And its the same case with the music as it was with the Looks: The Trailer sounded interesting, even on par with the Terraria OST, but ingame it just sounds lackluster. It sounds like something out of a freeware game handed out on one of those CDs that came bundled with German PC magazines back in the early 2000s. Weird comparison, i know. But thats the vibe im getting from it. It lacks personality.  [h1] The Gameplay: [/h1]  From what i was able to experience in this short amount of time it was "meh". I dont have a better word for it. Half of the time it was clicking through tutorials and dont knowing what you are doing, rest of the time it was aimlessly running through dungeons. Waves of enemys are spawning that are completely unsatisfying to kill. The Overworld is just boring. I dont know. I think it speaks for itself when i consider refunding the game after only half an hour.  That being said: I didnt do quests, i didnt get to see the management-"rimworldy" side of the game and i didnt leave the first island. So there are gameplay wise a bunch of things i did miss. It could be, that the game is totally worth it if you get the hang of things maybe 5 hours in. Who knows?   [hr][/hr]  [h1] Tl;Dr: [/h1]  I couldnt bring myself to play any more of this game. The characters look ugly and somehow have less personality than rimworld characters with its more than minimalistic artstyle. The UI is clunky, confusing and ugly. The Music lacks personality and tries to do what terraria does but fails. The Artstyle/Graphics/Look of the game is ugly and looks like something out of the default color palette of MS-Paint. I cant really judge the gameplay, but first impressions were'nt the best.  [b] Why this game is "Extremely Positive" is beyond me, i really cant explain it. Maybe because the game is cheap? Who knows... All i know for sure is, that within its current state i really can not recommend the game, even with it being on sale. [/b]  [h1] BUT [/h1]  I think there is real potential here. Maybe later on this game will morph into as much of a gem as terraria or rimworld. After all the concept is quite appealing and could be an absolute blast if done right. I wish the developer the best of luck, and i really hope he manages to make something big here.

It's the original version of Terraria with some village mechanics and worse graphics.  If you liked Terraria, you will probably like this, but it's going to need a lot of patches to be close to the same level.

Movement feels so slow it pains me.  Menu items are not scaled to resolution you'll have to lower your resolution in order to see anything going on.  Very promising, but those two things made me stop playing for now.  Maybe I'll suck it up one day.

It's EA, so issues are expected. But not when those issues make the core element of the gameplay redundant. For example - you can just steal loot from the chests of other villages. Nobody cares. And then use the looted items to convince the people from the town you just stole from to join yours. Why would I spend the time collecting sheep, then killing or shearing them for wool when I can just loot the next village of their chests?   Similarly, you can just use the first boss spawning item in the middle of someone else's village, and have their guards fight the boss for you. Then use the materials the boss drops to bypass the first 3-4 tiers of ore gear.   It has potential. Basically top down Terraria + Rimworld as others have said. However, those games have more than 20x the content Necesse has. At the moment, it feels like playing Terraria when it was first released. Which isn't a good thing, as they've almost copy and pasted the same weapons and items. Needs more variety in enemies, weapons, biomes and gameplay to be a yes from me. They'd do well to release modding early, before the hype dies down and people move on.

In its current state, I don't really see why you'd play this over Terraria Calamity. It's okay, but really lacks the polish that makes this sort of game satisfying. The movement feels slow and awkward, with nearly every boss moving significantly faster than you can even with a dash, making their patterns really awkward to maneouver around. The bosses themselves barely tie into progression so there's no incentive to fight them, and even when you do beat them, 80% of the rewards they drop are going to be useless to you, making the whole process feel entirely unrewarding.

EDIT: Original review below  I have recently tried this game again, and unfortunately I don't really recommend it anymore. Make no mistake, my original review still stands, but as the main criticisms I had have not been addressed, and instead been made objectively far worse in some degrees, i no longer have the impression that the game is going to become a particularly impressive product in the future. I will happily flip it right back around if the criticisms are addressed, because the rest of the game is perfectly competent, but I refuse to ignore these issues.  Movement speed is just still too slow overall, even when stacking speed it feels like you're only just on par. This basically invalidates any option that doesnt have the sprint. Invasions, while eased off a little in their danger against your villagers, are still just comedically unbalanced and broken beyond belief. Coupled with enemies tracking you with almost aimbot level precision and it just becomes an unfun mess of getting your ♥♥♥♥ stolen because you literally CAN NOT fight more than a few enemies at a time, even with OP weapons. Like, quarter the number of invaders or make it 1:1 to your settlement population, anything like that would be better than it is now. Or just make the majority of them melee. Honestly basic balancing.  In addition to that, basically every boss once you get into the deep caves is just garbage. The same five attacks over and over for a painfully boring amount of time, and they're either super easy because its skill based, or annoying and unfun because there's RNG you can do nothing about that screws you over. Just utter ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ design overall.   ---------------------------------------   I just wanna say that personally, I think this game has a rich of a future as something like Terraria (from which it is so clearly influenced by) as long as the devs continue to pour passion into it for years to come. Maybe not to the same time span as Terraria necessarily, but at least in quantity.  See, this game is very obviously a sort of top down terraria-esque game. I say that game specifically because there is massive amounts of evidence that the devs are at least a fan of Terraria and things like Calamity Mod because of the implementation of a number of ideas and design decisions. This also clearly came with some additional foresight of things that Terraria had worked on for years before nailing, as the Necesse devs added a number of nice ideas here and there that Terraria could honestly benefit from as well. Game progression is more or less the same as Terraria, too, as well as the way items work, are crafted, how fighting looks and sounds, all that stuff.   If you enjoy Terraria then this game is absolutely up your alley. Probably even moreso than something like Corekeeper, tbh.  That being said, this game is definitely an early version. Not that it's buggy (quite the opposite, actually. I never encountered a single one in my playtime so far) but rather just that it feels so incredibly 'initial'. Like, this isn't actually a bad thing assuming they address the problems, since I played the earliest public versions of Terraria I know how great things can become from such a humble beginning, but the issues are there in Necesse, and they should really be addressed.  Namely it's things like how travelling between islands kinda sucks. Always gotta float out or spend a scroll. Might benefit from the addition of warp pillars you can craft after finding stuff on other islands or something.   Also the movespeed is just too slow. Like, I get you can't really do stuff like grappling hooks or ninja gear or anything, but there is nothing less enjoyable than fighting a boss feeling like you're still moving as slow as you do right at the start of Terraria, and getting hit by some questionably designed boss mechanics simply because you couldn't move fast enough.  In addition to that, the raider attacks on your town scale quite aggressively despite your guards being borderline useless against anything other than the random night mobs.   So yeah, there's a number of other nitpicks I have, but this wasn't meant to tell you how bad or otherwise the game was, rather just to illustrate how currently unrefined the experience feels.  But again, games like Terraria and Binding of Isaac were no more polished when they first came out, so there's no reason Necesse can't follow the same path to glory.

TL;DR Its just top-down Terraria.   Necesse adds to the formula with Rimworld-esque settlement management. Settlers perform tasks like farming crops and hunting mobs while you're out adventuring as opposed to the static villagers in Terraria who do little more than act as merchants. IMO this doesn't really add much to the game. Sure, your home base is going to be more lively than in Terraria, settlers will pick stuff up for you and be more active during raid events, but the main focus of the game is exploration and loot progression so who cares? If I wanted to scratch the micromanaging, settlement building itch i'd play Rimworld.  I've already put in numerous hours in to Terraria so I was hoping for something more... game-changing. Overall the game is fine. However, due to the lack of innovation, I lost interest.  P.S. The artstyle is pretty bad... but OMG the music is awful, like migraine-inducing awful. Terraria's soundtrack slaps so that's another huge L in my books.

Soooo close... This is right on the fence, but I'm struggling to make myself open the game again since a big issue left such a sour taste in my mouth.   Early, the game is a lot of fun. Reminds me a lot of Terraria with the spelunking. The exploration, base building, farming, crafting, and recruiting of NPCs is all super great. I think you'll also get some enjoyment out of it for around 8-10 hours.  Then you hit the dungeon and it's just a drag, man. Enemies respawn SO fast it's struggle to even clear the dungeons. I put it on easy so I could steamroll the dungeon just because it was so terrible making an inch (literally) of progress at a time. I saw a handful of other reviews mention this, but I thought "They must be exaggerating!" No, they're not. It's that bad. All the fun I was having was instantly sucked out of the room when I hit that first dungeon.  The solution to this, other than putting it on easy, is to not fight anything. Run through the dungeon dropping torches every so often to stop so many enemies respawning, then clean up any enemies left. Only then can you actually explore without getting mobbed by 6 zombies at a time, only to kill them and have 5 more already spawned. They must respawn every 5-10 seconds!  This one thing really needs to be fixed, because the respawning is absolutely horrendous and made me not want to play anymore. If I see the respawns timers increased dramatically, I'll happily change this to recommended. I really want to play some more, but you've gotta go in dungeons to progress, and I just really don't want to step foot in another dungeon in this game.

As of writing I've completed all available content except one boss.  A lot of other reviews liken this to Terraria. I'm in agreement, which is why I'm not recommending the game.  Necesse is a solid survival adventure game with plenty of combat options, a decent town management system and perks, challenging boss fights, and thanks to the quest system, a relatively clear progression path. While it is clearly inspired by Terraria in much of its mechanics (four class system, trinkets and set bonuses, many of the items being effectively identical), it doesn't set itself apart far enough from Terraria that, given the similar prices, I'll say "Yeah, buy Necesse instead of Terraria." It learns from the pain points of Terraria, however, that I can see it being as good or better when it's complete, but not now.  The upsides: - Town system gives passive resources as settlers hunt, gather, or cook, which I'm a big fan of. - Cave exploration is given some variety while also trimming down on the number of different metals you need to work through, so you get to the fun bit (killing bosses) earlier. - Great inventory management, especially around crafting. Not needing a unified storage to effectively craft things is a godsend.  The neutrals: - Town management details are superficial. Some reviews compare this to Rimworld (???). Like Terraria, you can happily clear a run by locking your settlers in cells, but there's no "story" here like there might be in Rimworld. Neutral because while there's a happiness system, I'm not aware that it meaningfully affects gameplay. - Weapons have some variety but not a whole lot. Ironically melee weapons appear to have the greatest variety; magic weapons weapons, especially later on, fall into being either "instant beam" or "homing projectile"; ranged weapons are either "stronger bow" or "stronger gun"; and the only variation in summoning weapons are drops from the second-last tier of bosses.  The downsides: - The shift from platforming to top-down changes stat priority, and some of them can't be made up for. Summons fall very far behind the other classes within the last ~4 bosses available because they can't move fast enough to deal damage to bosses. Your movement options are extremely limited -- no wings+slime, a single dash, some minor speed boosts, and attacking slows you down. - UI is unrefined. Get used to grey and small, plain buttons. - An obvious one and maybe not fair, but content. Terraria is a complete game, it's as simple as that; not only, but there's a huge choice of mods available.  tl;dr: play Terraria and come back in a couple of years.
